2.note: tn Heb “set your face against.” The expression occurs at the beginning of Ezekiel’s prophetic oracles in Ezek 13:17; 20:46; 21:2; 25:2; 28:21; 29:2; 35:2; 38:2.sn Based on comparison to a similar expression in Ugaritic, the phrase may imply that Ezekiel was to actually go to these locations to deliver his message.

3.anchor: Say, ‘Mountains of Israel,
3.note: tn The phrase “mountains of Israel” occurs only in the book of Ezekiel (6:2, 3; 19:9; 33:28; 34:13, 14; 35:12; 36:1, 4, 8; 37:22; 38:8; 39:2, 4, 17). The expression refers to the whole land of Israel.sn The mountainous terrain of Israel would contrast with the exiles’ habitat in the river valley of Babylonia.
tn The introductory formula “Hear the word of the sovereign Lord” parallels a pronouncement delivered by the herald of a king (2 Kgs 18:28).

3.anchor: says to the mountains and the hills, to the ravines and the valleys: I am bringing
3.note: tn Heb “Look I, I am bringing.” The repetition of the pronoun draws attention to the speaker. The construction also indicates that the action is soon to come; the Lord is “about to bring a sword against” them.

3.anchor: a sword against you, and I will destroy your high places.
3.note: tn The Hebrew term refers to elevated platforms where pagan sacrifices were performed.

4.anchor: Your altars will be ruined and your incense altars will be broken. I will throw down your slain in front of your idols.
4.note: tn Thirty-nine of the forty-eight biblical occurrences of this Hebrew word are found in the book of Ezekiel.sn This verse is probably based on Lev 26:30 in which God forecasts that he will destroy their high places, cut off their incense altars, and set their corpses by the corpses of their idols.

5.anchor: I will place the corpses of the people of Israel in front of their idols,
5.note: tc This first sentence, which explains the meaning of the last sentence of the previous verse, does not appear in the LXX and may be an instance of a marginal explanatory note making its way into the text.

6.anchor: In all your dwellings, the cities will be laid waste and the high places ruined so that your altars will be laid waste and ruined, your idols will be shattered and demolished, your incense altars will be broken down, and your works wiped out.
6.note: tn The Hebrew verb translated “wiped out” is used to describe the judgment of the Flood (Gen 6:7; 7:4, 23).

7.note: sn The phrase you will know that I am the Lord concludes over sixty oracles in the book of Ezekiel and indicates the ultimate goal of God’s action. The phrase is often used in the book of Exodus as well (Exod 7:5; 14:4, 18). By Ezekiel’s day the people had forgotten that the Lord (Yahweh) was their covenant God and had turned to other gods. They had to be reminded that Yahweh alone deserved to be worshiped because only he possessed the power to meet their needs. Through judgment and eventually deliverance, Israel would be reminded that Yahweh alone held their destiny in his hands.

8.anchor: “‘But I will spare some of you. Some will escape the sword when you are scattered in foreign lands.
8.note: tn Heb “when you have fugitives from the sword among the nations, when you are scattered among the lands.”

9.anchor: Then your survivors will remember me among the nations where they are exiled. They will realize
9.note: tn The words “they will realize” are not in the Hebrew text; they are added here for stylistic reasons since this clause assumes the previous verb “to remember” or “to take into account.”

9.anchor: how I was crushed by their unfaithful
9.note: tn Heb “how I was broken by their adulterous heart.” The image of God being “broken” is startling, but perfectly natural within the metaphorical framework of God as offended husband. The idiom must refer to the intense grief that Israel’s unfaithfulness caused God. For a discussion of the syntax and semantics of the Hebrew text, see M. Greenberg, Ezekiel (AB), 1:134.

9.anchor: heart which turned from me and by their eyes which lusted after their idols. They will loathe themselves
9.note: tn Heb adds “in their faces.”

10.anchor: my threats to bring this catastrophe on them were not empty.’
10.note: tn Heb “not in vain did I speak to do to them this catastrophe.” The wording of the last half of v. 10 parallels God’s declaration after the sin of the golden calf (Exod 32:14).

11.anchor: says: Clap your hands, stamp your feet, and say, “Ah!” because of all the evil, abominable practices of the house of Israel, for they will fall by the sword, famine, and pestilence.
11.note: sn By the sword and by famine and by pestilence. A similar trilogy of punishments is mentioned in Lev 26:25-26. See also Jer 14:12; 21:9; 27:8, 13; 29:18).

12.anchor: The one far away will die by pestilence, the one close by will fall by the sword, and whoever is left and has escaped these
12.note: tn Heb “the one who is left, the one who is spared.”

13.anchor: – when their dead lie among their idols around their altars, on every high hill and all the mountaintops, under every green tree and every leafy oak,
13.note: sn By referring to every high hill…all the mountaintops…under every green tree and every leafy oak Ezekiel may be expanding on the phraseology of Deut 12:2 (see 1 Kgs 14:23; 2 Kgs 16:4; 17:10; Jer 2:20; 3:6, 13; 2 Chr 28:4).

14.anchor: I will stretch out my hand against them
14.note: sn I will stretch out my hand against them is a common expression in the book of Ezekiel (14:9, 13; 16:27; 25:7; 35:3).

14.anchor: and make the land a desolate waste from the wilderness to Riblah,
14.note: tc The Vulgate reads the name as “Riblah,” a city north of Damascus. The MT reads Diblah, a city otherwise unknown. The letters resh (ר) and dalet (ד) may have been confused in the Hebrew text. The town of Riblah was in the land of Hamath (2 Kgs 23:33) which represented the northern border of Israel (Ezek 47:14).

1.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man, eat what you see in front of you
1.note: tn Heb “eat what you find.”

3.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man, feed your stomach and fill your belly with this scroll I am giving to you.” So I ate it,
3.note: tc Heb “I ate,” a first common singular preterite plus paragogic he (ה). The ancient versions read “I ate it,” which is certainly the meaning in the context, and indicates they read the he as a third feminine singular pronominal suffix. The Masoretes typically wrote a mappiq in the he for the pronominal suffix but apparently missed this one.sn I ate it. A similar idea of consuming God’s word is found in Jer 15:16 and Rev 10:10, where it is also compared to honey and may be specifically reminiscent of this text.

5.anchor: For you are not being sent to a people of unintelligible speech
5.note: tn Heb “deep of lip” (in the sense of incomprehensible).

5.anchor: and difficult language,
5.note: tn Heb “heavy of tongue.” Similar language occurs in Exod 4:10; Isa 33:19.

5.anchor: but
5.note: tn The conjunction “but” is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied from the context.

6.anchor: not to many peoples of unintelligible speech and difficult language, whose words you cannot understand
6.note: tn Heb “hear.”

6.anchor: – surely if
6.note: tc The MT reads “if not” but most ancient versions translate only “if.” The expression occurs with this sense in Isa 5:9; 14:24. See also Ezek 34:8; 36:5; 38:19.

7.anchor: But the house of Israel is unwilling to listen to you,
7.note: sn Moses (Exod 3:19) and Isaiah (Isa 6:9-10) were also told that their messages would not be received.

7.anchor: because they are not willing to listen to me,
7.note: sn A similar description of Israel’s disobedience is given in 1 Sam 8:7.

7.anchor: for the whole house of Israel is hard-headed and hard-hearted.
7.note: tn Heb “hard of forehead and stiff of heart.”

8.anchor: “I have made your face adamant
8.note: tn Heb “strong, resolute.”

9.anchor: I have made your forehead harder than flint – like diamond!
9.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “diamond” is parallel to “iron” in Jer 17:1. The Hebrew uses two terms which are both translated at times as “flint,” but here one is clearly harder than the other. The translation “diamond” attempts to reflect this distinction in English.

9.anchor: Do not fear them or be terrified of the looks they give you,
9.note: tn Heb “of their faces.”

11.anchor: Go to the exiles, to your fellow countrymen,
11.note: tn Heb “to the sons of your people.”

12.anchor: Then a wind lifted me up
12.note: sn See note on “wind” in 2:2.

12.anchor: rose from its place,
12.note: tc This translation accepts the emendation suggested in BHS of בְּרוּם (bÿrum) for בָּרוּךְ (barukh). The letters mem (מ) and kaph (כ) were easily confused in the old script while בָּרוּךְ (“blessed be”) both implies a quotation which is out of place here and also does not fit the later phrase, “from its place,” which requires a verb of motion.

14.anchor: A wind lifted me up and carried me away. I went bitterly,
14.note: tn The traditional interpretation is that Ezekiel embarked on his mission with bitterness and anger, either reflecting God’s attitude toward the sinful people or his own feelings about having to carry out such an unpleasant task. L. C. Allen (Ezekiel [WBC], 1:13) takes “bitterly” as a misplaced marginal note and understands the following word, normally translated “anger,” in the sense of fervor or passion. He translates, “I was passionately moved” (p. 4). Another option is to take the word translated “bitterly” as a verb meaning “strengthened” (attested in Ugaritic). See G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 152.

14.anchor: rested powerfully
14.note: tn Heb “the hand of the Lord was on me heavily.” The “hand of the Lord” is a metaphor for his power or influence; the modifier conveys intensity. sn In Ezekiel God’s “hand” being on the prophet is regularly associated with communication or a vision from God (1:3; 3:14, 22; 8:1; 37:1; 40:1).

15.anchor: I came to the exiles at Tel Abib,
15.note: sn The name “Tel Abib” is a transliteration of an Akkadian term meaning “mound of the flood,” i.e., an ancient mound. It is not to be confused with the modern city of Tel Aviv in Israel.

15.anchor: who lived by the Kebar River.
15.note: tn Or “canal.”

15.anchor: I sat dumbfounded among them there, where they were living, for seven days.
15.note: sn A similar response to a divine encounter is found in Acts 9:8-9.

16.anchor: came to me:
16.note: sn This phrase occurs about fifty times in the book of Ezekiel.

17.anchor: “Son of man, I have appointed you a watchman
17.note: tn The literal role of a watchman is described in 2 Sam 18:24; 2 Kgs 9:17.

18.anchor: When I say to the wicked, “You will certainly die,”
18.note: sn Even though the infinitive absolute is used to emphasize the warning, the warning is still implicitly conditional, as the following context makes clear.

18.anchor: and you do not warn him – you do not speak out to warn the wicked to turn from his wicked deed and wicked lifestyle so that he may live – that wicked person will die for his iniquity,
18.note: tn Or “in his punishment.” The phrase “in/for [a person’s] iniquity” occurs fourteen times in Ezekiel: here and v. 19; 4:17; 7:13, 16; 18: 17, 18, 19, 20; 24:23; 33:6, 8, 9; 39:23. The Hebrew word for “iniquity” may also mean the “punishment for iniquity.”

18.anchor: but I will hold you accountable for his death.
18.note: tn Heb “his blood I will seek from your hand.” The expression “seek blood from the hand” is equivalent to requiring the death penalty (2 Sam 4:11-12).

19.anchor: But as for you, if you warn the wicked and he does not turn from his wicked deed and from his wicked lifestyle, he will die for his iniquity but you will have saved your own life.
19.note: tn Verses 17-19 are repeated in Ezek 33:7-9.

20.anchor: “When a righteous person turns from his righteousness and commits iniquity, and I set an obstacle
20.note: tn Or “stumbling block.” The Hebrew term refers to an obstacle in the road in Lev 19:14.

21.anchor: However, if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he
21.note: tn Heb “the righteous man.”

22.anchor: The hand
22.note: tn Or “power.” sn Hand in the OT can refer metaphorically to power, authority, or influence. In Ezekiel God’s hand being on the prophet is regularly associated with communication or a vision from God (1:3; 3:14, 22; 8:1; 37:1; 40:1).

22.anchor: rested on me there, and he said to me, “Get up, go out to the valley,
22.note: sn Ezekiel had another vision at this location, recounted in Ezek 37.

23.anchor: was standing there, just like the glory I had seen by the Kebar River,
23.note: tn Or “canal.”

24.anchor: Then a wind
24.note: tn See the note on “wind” in 2:2.

24.anchor: Lord
24.note: tn Heb “he.”

26.anchor: I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth so that you will be silent and unable to reprove
26.note: tn Heb “you will not be to them a reprover.” In Isa 29:21 and Amos 5:10 “a reprover” issued rebuke at the city gate.

27.anchor: But when I speak with you, I will loosen your tongue
says.’ Those who listen will listen, but the indifferent will refuse,
27.note: tn Heb “the listener will listen, the refuser will refuse.” Because the word for listening can also mean obeying, the nuance may be that the obedient will listen, or that the one who listens will obey. Also, although the verbs are not jussive as pointed in the MT, some translate them with a volitive sense: “the one who listens – let that one listen, the one who refuses – let that one refuse.”

1.anchor: “And you, son of man, take a brick
1.note: sn Ancient Near Eastern bricks were 10 to 24 inches long and 6 to 13 1/2 inches wide.

1.anchor: and set it in front of you. Inscribe
1.note: tn Or perhaps “draw.”

2.anchor: Lay siege to it! Build siege works against it. Erect a siege ramp
2.note: tn Or “a barricade.”

2.anchor: against it! Post soldiers outside it
2.note: tn Heb “set camps against it.”

3.anchor: Then for your part take an iron frying pan
3.note: tn Or “a griddle,” that is, some sort of plate for cooking.

3.anchor: and set it up as an iron wall between you and the city. Set your face toward it. It is to be under siege; you are to besiege it. This is a sign
3.note: tn That is, a symbolic object lesson.

4.anchor: “Also for your part lie on your left side and place the iniquity
4.note: tn Or “punishment” (also in vv. 5, 6).

5.anchor: I have determined that the number of the years of their iniquity are to be the number of days
5.note: tn Heb “I have assigned for you that the years of their iniquity be the number of days.” Num 14:33-34 is an example of the reverse, where the days were converted into years, the number of days spying out the land becoming the number of years of the wilderness wanderings.

5.anchor: for you – 390 days.
5.note: tc The LXX reads “190 days.” sn The significance of the number 390 is not clear. The best explanation is that “days” are used figuratively for years and the number refers to the years of the sinfulness of Israel during the period of the First Temple. Some understand the number to refer to the length of the division of the northern and southern kingdoms down to the fall of Jerusalem (931-586 b.c.), but this adds up to only 345 years.

5.anchor: So bear the iniquity of the house of Israel.
5.note: tn Or “When you have carried the iniquity of the house of Israel,” and continuing on to the next verse.

6.anchor: “When you have completed these days, then lie down a second time, but on your right side, and bear the iniquity of the house of Judah 40 days
6.note: sn The number 40 may refer in general to the period of Judah’s exile using the number of years Israel was punished in the wilderness. In this case, however, one would need to translate, “you will bear the punishment of the house of Judah.”

8.anchor: Look here, I will tie you up with ropes, so you cannot turn from one side to the other until you complete the days of your siege.
8.note: sn The action surely refers to a series of daily acts rather than to a continuous period.

9.anchor: “As for you, take wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt,
9.note: sn Wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt. All these foods were common in Mesopotamia where Ezekiel was exiled.

9.anchor: put them in a single container, and make food
9.note: tn Heb “bread.”

9.anchor: from them for yourself. For the same number of days that you lie on your side – 390 days
9.note: tc The LXX reads “190 days.”

10.anchor: The food you eat will be eight ounces
10.note: sn Eight ounces (Heb “twenty shekels”). The standards for weighing money varied considerably in the ancient Near East, but the generally accepted weight for the shekel is 11.5 grams (0.4 ounce). This makes the weight of grain about 230 grams here (8 ounces).

10.anchor: a day by weight; you must eat it at fixed
10.note: tn Heb “from time to time.”

11.anchor: And you must drink water by measure, a pint and a half;
11.note: sn A pint and a half [Heb “one-sixth of a hin”]. One-sixth of a hin was a quantity of liquid equal to about 1.3 pints or 0.6 liters.

12.anchor: And you must eat the food like you would a barley cake. You must bake it in front of them over a fire made with dried human excrement.”
12.note: sn Human waste was to remain outside the camp of the Israelites according to Deut 23:15.

13.anchor: said, “This is how the people of Israel will eat their unclean food among the nations
13.note: sn Unclean food among the nations. Lands outside of Israel were considered unclean (Josh 22:19; Amos 7:17).

14.anchor: , I have never been ceremonially defiled before. I have never eaten a carcass or an animal torn by wild beasts; from my youth up, unclean meat
14.note: tn The Hebrew term refers to sacrificial meat not eaten by the appropriate time (Lev 7:18; 19:7).

16.anchor: Then he said to me, “Son of man, I am about to remove the bread supply
16.note: tn Heb, “break the staff of bread.” The bread supply is compared to a staff that one uses for support.

16.anchor: in Jerusalem.
16.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

17.anchor: because they will lack bread and water. Each one will be terrified, and they will rot for their iniquity.
17.note: tn Or “in their punishment.” Ezek 4:16-17 alludes to Lev 26:26, 39. The phrase “in/for [a person’s] iniquity” occurs fourteen times in Ezekiel: here, 3:18, 19; 7:13, 16; 18: 17, 18, 19, 20; 24:23; 33:6, 8, 9; 39:23. The Hebrew word for “iniquity” may also mean the “punishment for iniquity.”

1.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man,
1.note: sn The phrase son of man occurs ninety-three times in the book of Ezekiel. It simply means “human one,” and distinguishes the prophet from the nonhuman beings that are present in the world of his vision.

2.note: tc The phrase “as he spoke to me” is absent from the LXX.

tn Or “spirit.” NIV has “the Spirit,” but the absence of the article in the Hebrew text makes this unlikely. Elsewhere in Ezekiel the Lord’s Spirit is referred to as “the Spirit of the Lord” (11:5; 37:1), “the Spirit of God” (11:24), or “my (that is, the Lord’s) Spirit” (36:27; 37:14; 39:29). Some identify the “spirit” of 2:2 as the spirit that energized the living beings, however, that “spirit” is called “the spirit” (1:12, 20) or “the spirit of the living beings” (1:20-21; 10:17). Still others see the term as referring to an impersonal “spirit” of strength or courage, that is, the term may also be understood as a disposition or attitude. The Hebrew word often refers to a wind in Ezekiel (1:4; 5:10, 12; 12:4; 13:11, 13; 17:10, 21; 19:12; 27:26; 37:9). In 37:5-10 a “breath” originates in the “four winds” and is associated with the Lord’s life-giving breath (see v. 14). This breath enters into the dry bones and gives them life. In a similar fashion the breath of 2:2 (see also 3:24) energizes paralyzed Ezekiel. Breath and wind are related. On the one hand it is a more normal picture to think of breath rather than wind entering someone, but since wind represents an external force it seems more likely for wind rather than breath to stand someone up (unless we should understand it as a disposition). It may be that one should envision the breath of the speaker moving like a wind to revive Ezekiel, helping him to regain his breath and invigorating him to stand. A wind also transports the prophet from one place to another (3:12, 14; 8:3; 11:1, 24; 43:5).

3.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man, I am sending you to the house
3.note: tc The Hebrew reads “sons of,” while the LXX reads “house,” implying the more common phrase in Ezekiel. Either could be abbreviated with the first letter ב (bet). In preparation for the characterization “house of rebellion,” in vv. 5, 6, and 8, “house” is preferred (L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 1:10 and W. Zimmerli, Ezekiel [Hermeneia], 2:564-65).

3.anchor: of Israel, to rebellious nations
3.note: tc Heb “to the rebellious nations.” The phrase “to the rebellious nations” is omitted in the LXX. Elsewhere in Ezekiel the singular word “nation” is used for Israel (36:13-15; 37:22). Here “nations” may have the meaning of “tribes” or refer to the two nations of Israel and Judah.

3.anchor: who have rebelled against me; both they and their fathers have revolted
3.note: tc This word is omitted from the LXX. tn The Hebrew term used here is the strongest word available for expressing a covenant violation. The word is used in the diplomatic arena to express a treaty violation (2 Kgs 1:1; 3:5, 7).

4.anchor: The people
4.note: tn Heb “sons.” The word choice may reflect treaty idiom, where the relationship between an overlord and his subjects can be described as that of father and son.

4.anchor: to whom I am sending you are obstinate and hard-hearted,
4.note: tc Heb “stern of face and hard of heart.” The phrases “stern of face” and “hard of heart” are lacking in the LXX.

4.anchor: says.’
4.note: tn The phrase “thus says [the Lord]” occurs 129 times in Ezekiel; the announcement is identical to the way messengers often introduced their messages (Gen 32:5; 45:9; Exod 5:10; Num 20:14; Judg 11:15).

5.anchor: And as for them,
5.note: tn Heb “they”; the phrase “And as for them” has been used in the translation for clarity.

5.anchor: whether they listen
5.note: tn The Hebrew word implies obedience rather than mere hearing or paying attention.

5.anchor: or not – for they are a rebellious
5.note: tn This Hebrew adjective is also used to describe the Israelites in Num 17:25 and Isa 30:9.

5.anchor: house
5.note: sn The book of Ezekiel frequently refers to the Israelites as a rebellious house (Ezek 2:5, 6, 8; 3:9, 26-27; 12:2-3, 9, 25; 17:12; 24:3).

6.anchor: But you, son of man, do not fear them, and do not fear their words – even though briers
6.note: tn The Hebrew term occurs only here in the OT.

6.anchor: and thorns
6.note: tn The Hebrew term is found elsewhere in the OT only in Ezek 28:24.sn Here thorns may be a figure for hostility (Ezek 28:24; Mic 7:4).

6.anchor: surround you and you live among scorpions – do not fear their words and do not be terrified of the looks they give you,
6.note: tn Heb “of their faces.”

10.anchor: He unrolled it before me, and it had writing on the front
10.note: tn Heb “on the face.”

10.anchor: and back;
10.note: sn Written on the front and back. While it was common for papyrus scrolls to have writing on both sides the same was not true for leather scrolls.

2.anchor: says to the land of Israel: An end! The end is coming on the four corners of the land!
2.note: tn Or “earth.” Elsewhere the expression “four corners of the earth” figuratively refers to the whole earth (Isa 11:12).

3.anchor: The end is now upon you, and I will release my anger against you; I will judge
3.note: tn Or “punish” (cf. BDB 1047 s.v. שָׁפַט 3.c).

3.anchor: you according to your behavior,
3.note: tn Heb “ways.”

3.anchor: I will hold you accountable for
3.note: tn Heb “I will place on you.”

4.anchor: My eye will not pity you; I will not spare
4.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term is primarily emotional: “to pity,” which in context implies an action, as in being moved by pity in order to spare them from the horror of their punishment.

4.anchor: you.
4.note: tn The pronoun “you” is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied.

4.anchor: For I will hold you responsible for your behavior,
4.note: tn “I will set your behavior on your head.”

4.anchor: and you will suffer the consequences of your abominable practices.
4.note: tn Heb “and your abominable practices will be among you.”

5.anchor: says: A disaster
5.note: tn The Hebrew term often refers to moral evil (see Ezek 6:10; 14:22), but in many contexts it refers to calamity or disaster, sometimes as punishment for evil behavior.

5.anchor: – a one-of-a-kind
5.note: tc So most Hebrew mss; many Hebrew mss read “disaster after disaster” (cf. NAB, NCV, NRSV, NLT).

6.anchor: An end comes
6.note: tn Or “has come.”

6.anchor: – the end comes!
6.note: tn Or “has come.”

6.anchor: It has awakened against you
6.note: tc With different vowels the verb rendered “it has awakened” would be the noun “the end,” as in “the end is upon you.” The verb would represent a phonetic wordplay. The noun by virtue of repetition would continue to reinforce the idea of the end. Whether verb or noun, this is the only instance to occur with this preposition.

6.anchor: – the end is upon you! Look, it is coming!
6.note: tc For this entire verse, the LXX has only “the end is come.” tn In each of the three cases of the verb translated with forms of “to come,” the form may either be a participle (“comes/is coming”) or a perfect (“has come”). Either form would indicate that the end is soon to arrive. This last form appears also to be feminine, although “end” is masculine. This shift may be looking ahead to the next verse, whose first noun (“Doom”) is feminine.

7.anchor: Doom is coming upon you who live in the land! The time is coming, the day
7.note: sn The day refers to the day of the Lord, a concept which, beginning in Amos 5:18-20, became a common theme in the OT prophetic books. It refers to a time when the Lord intervenes in human affairs as warrior and judge.

7.anchor: is near. There are sounds of tumult, not shouts of joy, on the mountains.
7.note: tc The LXX reads “neither tumult nor birth pains.” The LXX varies at many points from the MT in this chapter. The context suggests that one or both of these would be present on a day of judgment, thus favoring the MT. Perhaps more significant is the absence of “the mountains” in the LXX. If the ר (resh) in הָרִים (harim, “the mountains” not “on the mountains”) were a ד (dalet), which is a common letter confusion, then it could be from the same root as the previous word, הֵד (hed), meaning “the day is near – with destruction, not joyful shouting.”

8.anchor: Soon now I will pour out my rage
8.note: tn The expression “to pour out rage” also occurs in Ezek 9:8; 14:19; 20:8, 13, 21; 22:31; 30:15; 36:18.

9.anchor: My eye will not pity you; I will not spare
9.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term is primarily emotional: “to pity,” which in context implies an action, as in being moved by pity in order to spare them from the horror of their punishment.

9.anchor: you. For your behavior I will hold you accountable,
9.note: tn Heb “According to your behavior I will place on you.”

9.anchor: who is striking you.
9.note: tn The MT lacks “you.” It has been added for clarification.

11.anchor: Violence
11.note: tn Heb “the violence.”

11.anchor: has grown into a staff that supports wickedness. Not one of them will be left
11.note: tc The LXX reads “he will crush the wicked rod without confusion or haste.”tn The verb has been supplied for the Hebrew text to clarify the sense.

11.anchor: – not from their crowd, not from their wealth, not from their prominence.
11.note: tn The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT.

12.anchor: The time has come; the day has struck! The customer should not rejoice, nor the seller mourn; for divine wrath
12.note: tn Heb “wrath.” Context clarifies that God’s wrath is in view.

13.anchor: The customer will no longer pay the seller
13.note: tc The translation follows the LXX for the first line of the verse, although the LXX has lost the second line due to homoioteleuton (similar endings of the clauses). The MT reads “The seller will not return to the sale.” This Hebrew reading has been construed as a reference to land redemption, the temporary sale of the use of property, with property rights returned to the seller in the year of Jubilee. But the context has no other indicator that land redemption is in view. If correct, the LXX evidence suggests that one of the cases of “the customer” has been replaced by “the seller” in the MT, perhaps due to hoimoioarcton (similar beginnings of the words).

13.anchor: while both parties are alive, for the vision against their whole crowd
13.note: tn The Hebrew word refers to the din or noise made by a crowd, and by extension may refer to the crowd itself.

13.anchor: will not be revoked. Each person, for his iniquity,
13.note: tn Or “in their punishment.” The phrase “in/for [a person’s] iniquity” occurs fourteen times in Ezekiel: here and in v. 16; 3:18, 19; 4:17; 18:17, 18, 19, 20; 24:23; 33:6, 8, 9; 39:23. The Hebrew word for “iniquity” may also mean the “punishment for iniquity.”

14.anchor: “They have blown the trumpet and everyone is ready, but no one goes to battle, because my anger is against their whole crowd.
14.note: tn The Hebrew word refers to the din or noise made by a crowd, and by extension may refer to the crowd itself.

17.anchor: All of their hands will hang limp; their knees will be wet with urine.
17.note: tn Heb “their knees will run with water.” The expression probably refers to urination caused by fright, which is how the LXX renders the phrase. More colloquial English would simply be “they will wet their pants,” but as D. I. Block (Ezekiel [NICOT], 1:261, n. 98) notes, the men likely wore skirts which were short enough to expose urine on the knees.

18.anchor: They will wear sackcloth, terror will cover them; shame will be on all their faces, and all of their heads will be shaved bald.
18.note: tn Heb “baldness will be on their heads.”

19.anchor: They will discard their silver in the streets, and their gold will be treated like filth.
19.note: tn The Hebrew term can refer to menstrual impurity. The term also occurs at the end of v. 20.

19.anchor: ’s fury.
19.note: sn Compare Zeph 1:18.

19.anchor: They will not satisfy their hunger or fill their stomachs because their wealth
19.note: tn Heb “it.” Apparently the subject is the silver and gold mentioned earlier (see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 1:102).

19.anchor: was the obstacle leading to their iniquity.
19.note: tn The “stumbling block of their iniquity” is a unique phrase of the prophet Ezekiel (Ezek 14:3, 4, 7; 18:30; 44:12).

20.anchor: They rendered the beauty of his ornaments into pride,
20.note: tc The MT reads “he set up the beauty of his ornament as pride.” The verb may be repointed as plural without changing the consonantal text. The Syriac reads “their ornaments” (plural), implying עֶדְיָם (’edyam) rather than עֶדְיוֹ (’edyo) and meaning “they were proud of their beautiful ornaments.” This understands “ornaments” in the common sense of women’s jewelry, which then were used to make idols. The singular suffix “his ornaments” would refer to using items from the temple treasury to make idols. D. I. Block points out the foreshadowing of Ezek 16:17 which, with Rashi and the Targum, supports the understanding that this is a reference to temple items. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:265.

22.anchor: I will turn my face away from them and they will desecrate my treasured place.
22.note: sn My treasured place probably refers to the temple (however, cf. NLT “my treasured land”).

22.anchor: Vandals will enter it and desecrate it.
22.note: sn Since the pronouns “it” are both feminine, they do not refer to the masculine “my treasured place”; instead they probably refer to Jerusalem or the land, both of which are feminine in Hebrew.

23.anchor: (Make the chain,
23.note: tc The Hebrew word “the chain” occurs only here in the OT. The reading of the LXX (“and they will make carnage”) seems to imply a Hebrew text of ַהבַּתּוֹק (habbattoq, “disorder, slaughter”) instead of הָרַתּוֹק (haratoq, “the chain”). The LXX is also translating the verb as a third person plural future and taking this as the end of the preceding verse. As M. Greenberg (Ezekiel [AB], 1:154) notes, this may refer to a chain for a train of exiles but “the context does not speak of exile but of the city’s fall. The versions guess desperately and we can do little better.”

23.anchor: because the land is full of murder
23.note: tn Heb “judgment for blood,” i.e., indictment or accountability for bloodshed. The word for “judgment” does not appear in the similar phrase in 9:9.

24.anchor: I will bring the most wicked of the nations and they will take possession of their houses. I will put an end to the arrogance of the strong, and their sanctuaries
24.note: sn Or “their holy places” (KJV, ASV, NASB, NCV, NRSV).

25.anchor: Terror
25.note: tn The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT. It is interpreted based on a Syriac cognate meaning “to bristle or stiffen (in terror).”

27.anchor: The king will mourn and the prince will be clothed with shuddering; the hands of the people of the land will tremble. Based on their behavior I will deal with them, and by their standard of justice

1.anchor: “As for you, son of man, take a sharp sword and use it as a barber’s razor.
1.note: tn The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT.

1.anchor: Shave off some of the hair from your head and your beard.
1.note: tn Heb, “pass (it) over your head and your beard.”

3.anchor: But take a few strands of hair
3.note: tn Heb “from there a few in number.” The word “strands” has been supplied in the translation for clarification.

3.anchor: from those and tie them in the ends of your garment.
3.note: sn Objects could be carried in the end of a garment (Hag 2:12).

4.anchor: Again, take more of them and throw them into the fire,
4.note: tn Heb “into the midst of” (so KJV, ASV). This phrase has been left untranslated for stylistic reasons.

6.anchor: Then she defied my regulations and my statutes, becoming more wicked than the nations
6.note: sn The nations are subject to a natural law according to Gen 9; see also Amos 1:3-2:3; Jonah 1:2.

6.anchor: and the countries around her.
6.note: tn Heb “she defied my laws, becoming wicked more than the nations, and [she defied] my statutes [becoming wicked] more than the countries around her.”

6.anchor: Indeed, they
6.note: sn One might conclude that the subject of the plural verbs is the nations/countries, but the context (vv. 5-6a) indicates that the people of Jerusalem are in view. The text shifts from using the feminine singular (referring to personified Jerusalem) to the plural (referring to Jerusalem’s residents). See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 1:73.

7.anchor: says: Because you are more arrogant
7.note: tn Traditionally this difficult form has been derived from a hypothetical root הָמוֹן (hamon), supposedly meaning “be in tumult/uproar,” but such a verb occurs nowhere else. It is more likely that it is to be derived from a root מָנוֹן (manon), meaning “disdain” (see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 1:52). A derivative from this root is used in Prov 29:21 of a rebellious servant. See HALOT 600 s.v. מָנוֹן.

7.anchor: than the nations around you,
7.note: sn You are more arrogant than the nations around you. Israel is accused of being worse than the nations in Ezek 16:27; 2 Kgs 21:11; Jer 2:11.

7.anchor: you have not followed my statutes and have not carried out my regulations. You have not even
7.note: tc Some Hebrew mss and the Syriac omit the words “not even.” In this case they are being accused of following the practices of the surrounding nations. See Ezek 11:12.

8.anchor: says: I – even I – am against you,
8.note: tn Or “I challenge you.” The phrase “I am against you” may be a formula for challenging someone to combat or a duel. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:201-2, and P. Humbert, “Die Herausforderungsformel ‘h!nn#n' ?l?K>,’” ZAW 45 (1933): 101-8. The Hebrew text switches to a second feminine singular form here, indicating that personified Jerusalem is addressed (see vv. 5-6a). The address to Jerusalem continues through v. 15. In vv. 16-17 the second masculine plural is used, as the people are addressed.

8.anchor: and I will execute judgment
8.note: tn The Hebrew text uses wordplay here to bring out the appropriate nature of God’s judgment. “Execute” translates the same Hebrew verb translated “carried out” (literally meaning “do”) in v. 7, while “judgment” in v. 8 and “regulations” in v. 7 translate the same Hebrew noun (meaning “regulations” or in some cases “judgments” executed on those who break laws). The point seems to be this: God would “carry out judgments” against those who refused to “carry out” his “laws.”

8.anchor: among you while the nations watch.
8.note: tn Heb “in the sight of the nations.”sn This is one of the ironies of the passage. The Lord set Israel among the nations for honor and praise as they would be holy and obey God’s law as told in Ezek 5:5 and Deut 26:16-19. The practice of these laws and statutes would make the peoples consider Israel wise. (See Deut 4:5-8, where the words for laws and statutes are the same as those used here). Since Israel did not obey, they are made a different kind of object lesson to the nations, not by their obedience but in their punishment as told in Ezek 5:8 and Deut 29:24-29. Yet Deut 30 goes on to say that when they remember the cursings and blessings of the covenant and repent, God will restore them from the nations to which they have been scattered.

9.anchor: I will do to you what I have never done before and will never do again because of all your abominable practices.
9.note: tn Or “abominable idols.”

10.anchor: Therefore fathers will eat their sons within you, Jerusalem,
10.note: tn In context “you” refers to the city of Jerusalem. To make this clear for the modern reader, “Jerusalem” has been supplied in the translation in apposition to “you.”sn This cannibalism would occur as a result of starvation due to the city being besieged. It is one of the judgments threatened for a covenant law violation (Lev 26:29; see also Deut 28:53; Jer 19:9; Lam 2:20; Zech 11:9).

10.anchor: and sons will eat their fathers. I will execute judgments on you, and I will scatter any survivors
10.note: tn Heb “all of your survivors.”

10.anchor: to the winds.
10.note: tn Heb “to every wind.”

11.anchor: , because you defiled my sanctuary with all your detestable idols and with all your abominable practices, I will withdraw; my eye will not pity you, nor will I spare
11.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term is primarily emotional: “to pity,” which in context implies an action, as in being moved by pity in order to spare them from the horror of their punishment.

12.anchor: A third of your people will die of plague or be overcome by the famine within you.
12.note: sn The judgment of plague and famine comes from the covenant curse (Lev 26:25-26). As in v. 10, the city of Jerusalem is figuratively addressed here.

12.anchor: A third of your people will fall by the sword surrounding you,
12.note: sn Judgment by plague, famine, and sword occurs in Jer 21:9; 27:13; Ezek 6:11, 12; 7:15.

13.anchor: Then my anger will be fully vented; I will exhaust my rage on them, and I will be appeased.
13.note: tn Or “calm myself.”

13.anchor: , have spoken in my jealousy
13.note: tn The Hebrew noun translated “jealousy” is used in the human realm to describe suspicion of adultery (Num 5:14ff.; Prov 6:34). Since Israel’s relationship with God was often compared to a marriage this term is appropriate here. The term occurs elsewhere in Ezekiel in 8:3, 5; 16:38, 42; 23:25.

15.anchor: You will be
15.note: tc This reading is supported by the versions and by the Dead Sea Scrolls (11QEzek). Most Masoretic Hebrew mss read “it will be,” but if the final he (ה) is read as a mater lectionis, as it can be with the second masculine singular perfect, then they are in agreement. In either case the subject refers to Jerusalem.

15.anchor: an object of scorn and taunting,
15.note: tn The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT. A related verb means “revile, taunt” (see Ps 44:16).

15.anchor: a prime example of destruction
15.note: tn Heb “discipline and devastation.” These words are omitted in the Old Greek. The first term pictures Jerusalem as a recipient or example of divine discipline; the second depicts her as a desolate ruin (see Ezek 6:14).

15.anchor: among the nations around you when I execute judgments against you in anger and raging fury.
15.note: tn Heb “in anger and in fury and in rebukes of fury.” The heaping up of synonyms emphasizes the degree of God’s anger.

16.anchor: I will shoot against them deadly,
16.note: tn The Hebrew word carries the basic idea of “bad, displeasing, injurious,” but when used of weapons has the nuance “deadly” (see Ps 144:10).

16.anchor: destructive
16.note: tn Heb “which are/were to destroy.”

16.anchor: arrows of famine,
16.note: tn The language of this verse may have been influenced by Deut 32:23.

16.anchor: which I will shoot to destroy you.
16.note: tn Or “which were to destroy those whom I will send to destroy you” (cf. NASB).

16.anchor: I will prolong a famine on you and will remove the bread supply.
16.note: tn Heb, “break the staff of bread.” The bread supply is compared to a staff that one uses for support. See 4:16, as well as the covenant curse in Lev 26:26.

17.anchor: I will send famine and wild beasts against you and they will take your children from you.
17.note: tn Heb “will bereave you.”

17.anchor: Plague and bloodshed will overwhelm you,
17.note: tn Heb “will pass through you.” This threat recalls the warning of Lev 26:22, 25 and Deut 32:24-25.

1.anchor: In the sixth year, in the sixth month, on the fifth of the month,
1.note: tc The LXX reads “In the sixth year, in the fifth month, on the fifth of the month.” sn In the sixth year, in the sixth month, on the fifth of the month would be September 17, 592 b.c., about fourteen months after the initial vision.

1.anchor: as I was sitting in my house with the elders of Judah sitting in front of me, the hand
1.note: tn Or “power.” sn Hand in the OT can refer metaphorically to power, authority, or influence. In Ezekiel God’s hand being on the prophet is regularly associated with communication or a vision from God (3:14, 22; 8:1; 37:1; 40:1).

1.anchor: seized me.
1.note: tn Heb “fell upon me there,” that is, God’s influence came over him.

2.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates becoming aware of something and has been translated here as a verb (so also throughout the chapter).

2.anchor: a form that appeared to be a man.
2.note: tc The MT reads “fire” rather than “man,” the reading of the LXX. The nouns are very similar in Hebrew.

2.anchor: From his waist downward was something like fire,
2.note: tc The MT reads “what appeared to be his waist and downwards was fire.” The LXX omits “what appeared to be,” reading “from his waist to below was fire.” Suggesting that “like what appeared to be” belongs before “fire,” D. I. Block (Ezekiel [NICOT], 1:277) points out the resulting poetic symmetry of form with the next line as followed in the translation here.

2.anchor: and from his waist upward something like a brightness,
2.note: tc The LXX omits “like a brightness.”
tn See Ezek 1:4.

3.anchor: He stretched out the form
3.note: tn The Hebrew term is normally used as an architectural term in describing the pattern of the tabernacle or temple or a representation of it (see Exod 25:8; 1 Chr 28:11).

3.anchor: of a hand and grabbed me by a lock of hair on my head. Then a wind
3.note: tn Or “spirit.” See note on “wind” in 2:2.

3.anchor: lifted me up between the earth and sky and brought me to Jerusalem
3.note: map For the location of Jerusalem see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

3.anchor: by means of divine visions, to the door of the inner gate which faces north where the statue
3.note: tn Or “image.”

5.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man, look up toward
5.note: tn Heb “lift your eyes (to) the way of.”

6.anchor: He said to me, “Son of man, do you see what they are doing – the great abominations that the people
6.note: tn Heb “house.”

10.anchor: So I went in and looked. I noticed every figure
10.note: tn Or “pattern.”

10.anchor: of creeping thing and beast – detestable images
10.note: tn Heb “detestable.” The word is often used to describe the figures of foreign gods.

10.anchor: – and every idol of the house of Israel, engraved on the wall all around.
10.note: sn These engravings were prohibited in the Mosaic law (Deut 4:16-18).

11.anchor: Seventy men from the elders of the house of Israel
11.note: sn Note the contrast between these seventy men who represented Israel and the seventy elders who ate the covenant meal before God, inaugurating the covenant relationship (Exod 24:1, 9).

11.anchor: (with Jaazaniah son of Shaphan standing among them) were standing in front of them, each with a censer in his hand, and fragrant
11.note: tn The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT.

12.anchor: He said to me, “Do you see, son of man, what the elders of the house of Israel are doing in the dark, each in the chamber of his idolatrous images?
12.note: tn Heb “the room of his images.” The adjective “idolatrous” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.sn This type of image is explicitly prohibited in the Mosaic law (Lev 26:1).

14.anchor: ’s house. I noticed
14.note: tn Given the context this could be understood as a shock, e.g., idiomatically “Good grief! I saw….”

14.anchor: women sitting there weeping for Tammuz.
14.note: sn The worship of Tammuz included the observation of the annual death and descent into the netherworld of the god Dumuzi. The practice was observed by women in the ancient Near East over a period of centuries.

16.anchor: ’s house. Right there
16.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates becoming aware of something.

16.anchor: ’s temple, between the porch and the altar,
16.note: sn The priests prayed to God between the porch and the altar on fast days (Joel 2:17). This is the location where Zechariah was murdered (Matt 23:35).

16.anchor: were about twenty-five
16.note: tc The LXX reads “twenty” instead of twenty-five, perhaps because of the association of the number twenty with the Mesopotamian sun god Shamash. tn Or “exactly twenty-five.”

16.anchor: ’s temple,
16.note: sn The temple faced east.

16.anchor: facing east – they were worshiping the sun
16.note: tn Or “the sun god.” sn The worship of astral entities may have begun during the reign of Manasseh (2 Kgs 21:5).

17.anchor: He said to me, “Do you see, son of man? Is it a trivial thing that the house of Judah commits these abominations they are practicing here? For they have filled the land with violence and provoked me to anger still further. Look, they are putting the branch to their nose!
17.note: tn It is not clear what the practice of “holding a branch to the nose” indicates. A possible parallel is the Syrian relief of a king holding a flower to his nose as he worships the stars (ANEP 281). See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 1:145-46. The LXX glosses the expression as “Behold, they are like mockers.”

18.anchor: Therefore I will act with fury! My eye will not pity them nor will I spare
18.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term is primarily emotional: “to pity,” which in context implies an action, as in being moved by pity in order to spare them from the horror of their punishment.

1.anchor: In the thirtieth year,
1.note: sn The meaning of the thirtieth year is problematic. Some take it to mean the age of Ezekiel when he prophesied (e.g., Origen). The Aramaic Targum explains the thirtieth year as the thirtieth year dated from the recovery of the book of the Torah in the temple in Jerusalem (2 Kgs 22:3-9). The number seems somehow to be equated with the fifth year of Jehoiachin’s exile in 1:2, i.e., 593 b.c.

1.anchor: on the fifth day of the fourth month, while I was among the exiles
1.note: sn The Assyrians started the tactic of deportation, the large-scale forced displacement of conquered populations, in order to stifle rebellions. The task of uniting groups of deportees, gaining freedom from one’s overlords and returning to retake one’s own country would be considerably more complicated than living in one’s homeland and waiting for an opportune moment to drive out the enemy’s soldiers. The Babylonians adopted this practice also, after defeating the Assyrians. The Babylonians deported Judeans on three occasions. The practice of deportation was reversed by the Persian conquerors of Babylon, who gained favor from their subjects for allowing them to return to their homeland and, as polytheists, sought the favor of the gods of the various countries which had come under their control.

1.anchor: at the Kebar River,
1.note: sn The Kebar River is mentioned in Babylonian texts from the city of Nippur in the fifth century b.c. It provided artificial irrigation from the Euphrates.

1.anchor: the heavens opened
1.note: sn For the concept of the heavens opened in later literature, see 3 Macc 6:18; 2 Bar. 22:1; T. Levi 5:1; Matt 3:16; Acts 7:56; Rev 19:11.

1.anchor: and I saw a divine vision.
1.note: tn Or “saw visions from God.” References to divine visions occur also in Ezek 8:3; 40:2

3.anchor: came to the priest Ezekiel
3.note: sn The prophet’s name, Ezekiel, means in Hebrew “May God strengthen.”

3.anchor: the son of Buzi,
3.note: tn Or “to Ezekiel son of Buzi the priest.”

3.anchor: at the Kebar River in the land of the Babylonians.
3.note: tn Heb “Chaldeans.” The name of the tribal group ruling Babylon, “Chaldeans” is used as metonymy for the whole empire of Babylon. The Babylonians worked with the Medes to destroy the Assyrian Empire near the end of the 7th century b.c. Then, over the next century, the Babylonians dominated the West Semitic states (such as Phoenicia, Aram, Moab, Edom, and Judah in the modern countries of Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Israel) and made incursions into Egypt.

3.anchor: The hand
3.note: tn Or “power.” sn Hand in the OT can refer metaphorically to power, authority, or influence. In Ezekiel God’s “hand” being on the prophet is regularly associated with communication or a vision from God (3:14, 22; 8:1; 37:1; 40:1).

4.anchor: As I watched, I noticed
4.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates becoming aware of something and has been translated here as a verb.

4.anchor: a windstorm
4.note: sn Storms are often associated with appearances of God (see Nah 1:3; Ps 18:12). In some passages, the “storm” (סְעָרָה, sÿ’arah) may be a whirlwind (Job 38:1, 2 Kgs 2:1).

4.anchor: coming from the north – an enormous cloud, with lightning flashing,
4.note: tn Heb “fire taking hold of itself,” perhaps repeatedly. The phrase occurs elsewhere only in Exod 9:24 in association with a hailstorm. The LXX interprets the phrase as fire flashing like lightning, but it is possibly a self-sustaining blaze of divine origin. The LXX also reverses the order of the descriptors, i.e., “light went around it and fire flashed like lightning within it.”

4.anchor: such that bright light
4.note: tn Or “radiance.” The term also occurs in 1:27b.

4.anchor: rimmed it and came from
4.note: tc Or “was in it”; cf. LXX ἐν τῷ μέσῳ αὐτοῦ (en tw mesw autou, “in its midst”).

4.anchor: it like glowing amber
4.note: tn The LXX translates חַשְׁמַל (khashmal) with the word ἤλεκτρον (hlektron, “electrum”; so NAB), an alloy of silver and gold, perhaps envisioning a comparison to the glow of molten metal.

5.anchor: In the fire
5.note: tc Heb “from its midst” (מִתּוֹכָהּ, mitokhah). The LXX reads ἐν τῷ μέσῳ (en tw mesw, “in the midst of it”). The LXX also reads ἐν for מִתּוֹךְ (mitokh) in v. 4. The translator of the LXX of Ezekiel either read בְּתוֹךְ (bÿtokh, “within”) in his Hebrew exemplar or could not imagine how מִתּוֹךְ could make sense and so chose to use ἐν. The Hebrew would be understood by adding “from its midst emerged the forms of four living beings.”

5.anchor: were what looked like
5.note: tn Heb “form, figure, appearance.”

5.anchor: four living beings.
5.note: tn The Hebrew term is feminine plural yet thirty-three of the forty-five pronominal suffixes and verbal references which refer to the living beings in the chapter are masculine plural. The grammatical vacillation between masculine and feminine plurals suggests the difficulty Ezekiel had in penning these words as he was overcome by the vision of God. In ancient Near Eastern sculpture very similar images of part-human, part-animal creatures serve as throne and sky bearers. For a discussion of ancient Near Eastern parallels, see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 1:26-31. Ezekiel’s vision is an example of contextualization, where God accommodates his self-revelation to cultural expectations and norms.

5.anchor: In their appearance they had human form,
5.note: sn They had human form may mean they stood erect.

7.anchor: Their legs were straight, but the soles of their feet were like calves’ feet. They gleamed
7.note: sn The Hebrew verb translated gleamed occurs only here in the OT.

8.anchor: They had human hands
8.note: tc The MT reads “his hand” while many Hebrew mss as well as the Qere read “hands of.” Two similar Hebrew letters, vav and yod, have been confused.

9.anchor: their wings touched each other; they did not turn as they moved, but went straight ahead.
9.note: tn Heb “They each went in the direction of one of his faces.”

10.anchor: Their faces had this appearance: Each of the four had the face of a man, with the face of a lion on the right, the face of an ox on the left and also the face of an eagle.
10.note: tc The MT has an additional word at the beginning of v. 11, וּפְנֵיהֶם (ufÿnehem, “and their faces”), which is missing from the LXX. As the rest of the verse only applies to wings, “their faces” would have to somehow be understood in the previous clause. But this would be very awkward and is doubly problematic since “their faces” are already introduced as the topic at the beginning of v. 10. The Hebrew scribe appears to have copied the phrase “and their faces and their wings” from v. 8, where it introduces the content of 9-11. Only “and (as for) their wings” belongs here.

12.anchor: Each moved straight ahead
12.note: tn See the note on “straight ahead” in v. 9.

12.anchor: – wherever the spirit
12.note: tn Or “wind.”

13.anchor: In the middle
13.note: tc The MT reads “and the form of the creatures” (וּדְמוּת הַחַיּוֹת, udÿmut hakhayyot). The LXX reads “and in the midst of the creatures,” suggesting an underlying Hebrew text of וּמִתּוֹךְ הַחַיּוֹת (umittokh hakhayyot). The subsequent description of something moving among the creatures supports the LXX.

13.anchor: of the living beings was something like

18.anchor: Their rims were high and awesome,
18.note: tc The MT reads וְיִרְאָה לָהֶם (vÿyir’ah lahem, “and fear belonged to them”). In a similar vision in 10:12 the wheels are described as having spokes (יִדֵיהֶם, yideyhem). That parallel would suggest יָדוֹת (yadot) here (written יָדֹת without the mater). By positing both a ד/ר (dalet/resh) confusion and a ת/ה (hey/khet) confusion the form was read as וְיָרֵה (vÿyareh) and was then misunderstood and subsequently written as וְיִרְאָה (vÿyir’ah) in the MT. The reading וְיִרְאָה does not seem to fit the context well, though in English it can be made to sound as if it does. See W. H. Brownlee, Ezekiel 1-19 (WBC), 8-9. The LXX reads καὶ εἶδον αὐτά (kai eidon auta, “and I saw”), which assumes וָאֵרֶא (va’ere’). The existing consonants of the MT may also be read as “it was visible to them.”

9.anchor: , have made a fool of
9.note: tn The translation is uncertain due to difficulty both in determining the meaning of the verb’s stem and its conjugation in this context. In the Qal stem the basic meaning of the verbal root פָּתַה (patah) is “to be gullible, foolish.” The doubling stems (the Pual and Piel used in this verse) typically give such stative verbs a factitive sense, hence either “make gullible” (i.e., “entice”) or “make into a fool” (i.e., “to show to be a fool”). The latter represents the probable meaning of the term in Jer 20:7, 10 and is followed here (see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 1:193; R. Mosis “Ez 14, 1-11 - ein Ruf zur Umkehr,” BZ 19 [1975]: 166-69 and ThWAT 4:829-31). In this view, if a prophet speaks when not prompted by God, he will be shown to be a fool, but this does not reflect negatively on the Lord because it is God who shows him to be a fool. Secondly, the verb is in the perfect conjugation and may be translated “I have made a fool of him” or “I have enticed him,” or to show determination (see IBHS 439-41 §27.2f and g), or in certain syntactical constructions as future. Any of these may be plausible if the doubling stems used are understood in the sense of “making a fool of.” But if understood as “to make gullible,” more factors come into play. As the Hebrew verbal form is a perfect, it is often translated as present perfect: “I have enticed.” In this case the Lord states that he himself enticed the prophet to cooperate with the idolaters. Such enticement to sin would seem to be a violation of God’s moral character, but sometimes he does use such deception and enticement to sin as a form of punishment against those who have blatantly violated his moral will (see, e.g., 2 Sam 24). If one follows this line of interpretation in Ezek 14:9, one would have to assume that the prophet had already turned from God in his heart. However, the context gives no indication of this. Therefore, it is better to take the perfect as indicating certitude and to translate it with the future tense: “I will entice.” In this case the Lord announces that he will judge the prophet appropriately. If a prophet allows himself to be influenced by idolaters, then the Lord will use deception as a form of punishment against that deceived prophet. A comparison with the preceding oracles also favors this view. In 14:4 the perfect of certitude is used for emphasis (see “I will answer”), though in v. 7 a participle is employed. For a fuller discussion of this text, see R. B. Chisholm, Jr., “Does God Deceive?” BSac 155 (1998): 23-25.

10.anchor: says: The prince will raise this burden in Jerusalem,
10.note: tc The nearly incoherent Hebrew reads “The prince is this burden (prophetic oracle?) in Jerusalem.” The Targum, which may only be trying to make sense of a very difficult text, says “Concerning the prince is this oracle,” assuming the addition of a preposition. This would be the only case where Ezekiel uses this term for a prophetic oracle. The LXX reads the word for “burden” as a synonym for leader, as both words are built on the same root (נָשִׂיא, nasi’), but the verse is still incoherent because it is only a phrase with no verb. The current translation assumes that the verb יִשָּׂא (yisa’) from the root נָשִׂיא has dropped out due to homoioteleuton. If indeed the verb has dropped out (the syntax of the verbless clause being the problem), then context clearly suggests that it be a form of נָשִׂיא (see vv. 7 and 12). Placing the verb between the subject and object would result in three consecutive words based on the root נָשִׂיא and an environment conducive to an omission in copying: הַנָּשִׂיא יִשָּׁא הַמַּשָּׂא הַזֶּה (hannasi’ yisha’ hammasa’ hazzeh, “the Prince will raise this burden”). sn The prince in Jerusalem refers to King Zedekiah.map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

25.anchor: them decrees
25.note: tn The Hebrew term חֻקּוֹת (khuqot; translated “statutes” elsewhere in this chapter) is normally feminine. Here Ezekiel changes the form to masculine: חֻקִּים (khuqim). Further, they are not called “my decrees” as vv. 11 and 13 refer to “my statutes.” The change is a signal that Ezekiel is not talking about the same statutes in vv. 11 and 13, which lead to life.

26.note: sn This act is prohibited in Deut 12:29-31 and Jer 7:31; 19:5; 32:35. See also 2 Kgs 21:6; 23:10. This custom indicates that the laws the Israelites were following were the disastrous laws of pagan nations (see Ezek 16:20-21).

sn God sometimes punishes sin by inciting the sinner to sin even more, as the biblical examples of divine hardening and deceit make clear. See Robert B. Chisholm, Jr., “Divine Hardening in the Old Testament,” BSac 153 (1996): 410-34; idem, “Does God Deceive?” BSac 155 (1998): 11-28. For other instances where the Lord causes individuals to act unwisely or even sinfully as punishment for sin, see 1 Sam 2:25; 2 Sam 17:14; 1 Kgs 12:15; 2 Chr 25:20.

7.note: tc The Hebrew text reads “knew,” but is apparently the result of a ר-ד (dalet-resh) confusion. For a defense of the emendation, see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 1:284. However, Allen retains the reading “widows” as the object of the verb, which he understands in the sense of “do harm to,” and translates the line: “He did harm to women by making them widows” (p. 282). The line also appears to be lacking a beat for the meter of the poem.

7.note: tc The Hebrew text reads “widows” instead of “strongholds,” apparently due to a confusion of ר (resh) and ל (lamed). L. C. Block, (Ezekiel [NICOT], 1:602) also defends the Hebrew text, arguing that the image is that of a dominant male lion who takes over the pride and by copulating with the females lays claim to his predecessor’s “widows.”

2.anchor: “As for you, son of man, are you willing to pronounce judgment,
2.note: tn Heb “will you judge.” Here the imperfect form of the verb is probably used with a desiderative nuance. Addressed to the prophet, “judge” means to warn of or pronounce God’s impending judgment upon the city. See 20:4.

2.anchor: are you willing to pronounce judgment on the bloody city?
2.note: tn The phrase “bloody city” is used of Nineveh in Nah 3:1.

3.anchor: says: O city, who spills blood within herself (which brings on her doom),
3.note: tn Heb “her time”; this refers to the time of impending judgment (see the note on “doom” in v. 4).

4.anchor: you are guilty because of the blood you shed and defiled by the idols you made. You have hastened the day of your doom;
4.note: tn Heb “you have brought near your days.” The expression “bring near your days” appears to be an adaptation of the idiom “days draw near,” which is used to indicate that an event, such as death, is imminent (see Gen 27:41; 47:29; Deut 31:14; 1 Kgs 2:1; Ezek 12:23). Here “your days” probably refers to the days of the personified city’s life, which was about to come to an end through God’s judgment.

4.anchor: the end of your years has come.
4.note: tn Heb “and you have come to your years.” This appears to mean that she has arrived at the time when her years (i.e., life) would end, though it may mean that her years of punishment will begin. Because “day” and “time” are so closely associated in the immediate context (see 21:25, 29) some prefer to emend the text and read “you have brought near your time.” See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:31, as well as the translator’s note on verse 3.

4.anchor: Therefore I will make
4.note: tn The Hebrew verb is a prophetic perfect, emphasizing that the action is as good as done from the speaker’s perspective.

5.note: tn Heb “unclean of name.”

7.anchor: They have treated father and mother with contempt
7.note: tn Heb “treated lightly, cursed.”

7.anchor: within you; they have oppressed the foreigner among you; they have wronged the orphan and the widow
7.note: tn Widows and orphans are often coupled together in the OT (Deut 14:29; 16:11, 14; 24:19-21; 26:12-13; Jer 7:6; 22:3). They represented all who were poor and vulnerable to economic exploitation.

9.anchor: Slanderous men shed blood within you.
9.note: tn Heb “men of slander are in you in order to shed blood.”

9.anchor: Those who live within you eat pagan sacrifices on the mountains;
9.note: tn Heb “and on the mountains they eat within you.” The mountains mentioned here were the site of pagan sacrifices. See 18:6.

9.anchor: they commit obscene acts among you.
9.note: sn This statement introduces vv. 10-11 and refers in general terms to the sexual sins described there. For the legal background of vv. 10-11, see Lev 18:7-20; 20:10-21; Deut 22:22-23, 30; 27:22.

10.anchor: They have sex with their father’s wife within you;
10.note: tn Heb “the nakedness of a father one uncovers within you.” The ancient versions read the verb as plural (“they uncover”). If the singular is retained, it must be taken as indefinite and representative of the entire group. The idiomatic expression “uncover the nakedness” refers here to sexual intercourse (cf. Lev 18:6). To uncover a father’s nakedness could include sexual relations with one’s own mother (Lev 18:7), but more likely it refers to having intercourse with another wife of one’s father, such as a stepmother (Lev 18:8; cf. Gen 35:22; 49:4).

10.anchor: they violate women during their menstrual period within you.
10.note: tn Heb “(one who is) unclean due to the impurity they humble within you.” The use of the verb “to humble” suggests that these men forced themselves upon women during menstruation. Having sexual relations with a woman during her period was forbidden by the Law (Lev 18:19; 20:18).

11.anchor: One

11.note: sn Sexual relations with one’s half-sister may be primarily in view here. See Lev 18:9; 20:17.

12.note: tn Heb “usury and interest you take.” See 18:13, 17. This kind of economic exploitation violated the law given in Lev 25:36.

24.anchor: “Son of man, say to her: ‘You are a land that receives no rain
24.note: tc The MT reads “that is not cleansed”; the LXX reads “that is not drenched,” which assumes a different vowel pointing as well as the loss of a מ (mem) due to haplography. In light of the following reference to showers, the reading of the LXX certainly fits the context well. For a defense of the emendation, see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:32. Yet the MT is not an unreasonable reading since uncleanness in the land also fits the context, and a poetic connection between rain and the land being uncleansed may be feasible since washing with water is elsewhere associated with cleansing (Num 8:7; 31:23; Ps 51:7).

24.anchor: or showers in the day of my anger.’
24.note: tn Heb “in a day of anger.”

25.anchor: Her princes
25.note: tn Heb “a conspiracy of her prophets is in her midst.” The LXX reads “whose princes” rather than “a conspiracy of prophets.” The prophets are mentioned later in the paragraph (v. 28). If one follows the LXX in verse 25, then five distinct groups are mentioned in vv. 25-29: princes, priests, officials, prophets, and the people of the land. For a defense of the Septuagintal reading, see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:32, and D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:720, n. 4.
