17.note: tn Heb “the bread of men.” The translation follows the suggestion accepted by M. Greenberg (Ezekiel [AB], 2:509) that this refers to a meal brought by comforters to the one mourning. Some repoint the consonantal text to read “the bread of despair” (see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 2:56), while others, with support from the Targum and Vulgate, emend the consonantal text to read “the bread of mourners” (see D. I. Block, Ezekiel [NICOT], 1:784).

18.anchor: So I spoke to the people in the morning, and my wife died in the evening. In the morning
18.note: tn This may refer to the following morning. For a discussion of various interpretive options in understanding the chronology reflected in verse 18, see D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:790.

21.anchor: says: Realize I am about to desecrate my sanctuary – the source of your confident pride,
21.note: tn Heb “the pride of your strength” means “your strong pride.”

21.anchor: the object in which your eyes delight,
21.note: sn Heb “the delight of your eyes.” Just as Ezekiel was deprived of his beloved wife (v. 16, the “desire” of his “eyes”) so the Lord would be forced to remove the object of his devotion, the temple, which symbolized his close relationship to his covenant people.

21.anchor: and your life’s passion.
21.note: tn Heb “the object of compassion of your soul.” The accentuation in the traditional Hebrew text indicates that the descriptive phrases (“the source of your confident pride, the object in which your eyes delight, and your life’s passion”) modify the preceding “my sanctuary.”

21.anchor: Your very own sons and daughters whom you have left behind will die
21.note: tn Heb “fall.”

22.anchor: Then you will do as I have done: You will not cover your lip or eat food brought by others.
22.note: tn See v. 17.

23.anchor: Your turbans will be on your heads and your sandals on your feet; you will not mourn or weep, but you will rot
23.note: tn The same verb appears in 4:17 and 33:10.

23.anchor: for your iniquities
23.note: tn Or “in your punishment.” The phrase “in/for [a person’s] iniquity/punishment” occurs fourteen times in Ezekiel: here; 3:18, 19; 4:17; 7:13, 16; 18:17, 18, 19, 20; 33:6, 8, 9; 39:23. The Hebrew word for “iniquity” may also mean the “punishment” for iniquity or “guilt” of iniquity.

25.anchor: “And you, son of man, this is what will happen on the day I take
25.note: tn Heb “(Will) it not (be) in the day I take?”

25.anchor: from them their stronghold – their beautiful source of joy, the object in which their eyes delight, and the main concern of their lives,
25.note: tn Heb “the uplifting of their soul.” According to BDB 672 s.v. מַשָּׂא 2, the term “uplifting” refers to “that to which they lift up their soul, their heart’s desire.” However, this text is the only one listed for this use. It seems more likely that the term has its well-attested nuance of “burden, load,” here and refers to that which weighs them down emotionally and is a constant source of concern or worry.

25.anchor: as well as their sons and daughters:
25.note: tn In the Hebrew text there is no conjunction before “their sons and daughters.” For this reason one might assume that the preceding descriptive phrases refer to the sons and daughters, but verse 21 suggests otherwise. The descriptive phrases appear to refer to the “stronghold,” which parallels “my sanctuary” in verse 21. The children constitute a separate category.

26.anchor: On that day a fugitive will come to you to report the news.
26.note: tn Heb “to make the ears hear.”

27.anchor: On that day you will be able to speak again;

2.anchor: “Son of man, of all the woody branches among the trees of the forest, what happens to the wood of the vine?
2.note: tn Most modern translations take the statement as a comparison (“how is vine wood better than any forest wood?”) based on the preposition מִן (min). But a comparison should have a word as an adjective or stative verb designating a quality, i.e., a word for “good/better” is lacking. The preposition is translated above in its partitive sense.sn Comparing Israel to the wood of the vine may focus on Israel’s inferiority to the other nations. For the vine imagery in relation to Israel and the people of God, see Ps 80:8-13; John 15:1-7; Rom 11:17-22.

4.anchor: No!
4.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) draws one’s attention to something. Sometimes it may be translated as a verb of perception; here it is treated as a particle that fits the context (so also in v. 5, but with a different English word).

6.anchor: says: Like the wood of the vine is among the trees of the forest which I have provided as fuel for the fire – so I will provide the residents of Jerusalem
6.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

6.anchor: as fuel.
6.note: tn The words “as fuel” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied.

7.anchor: I will set
7.note: tn The word translated “set” is the same Hebrew word translated as “provide” in the previous verse.

7.anchor: my face against them – although they have escaped from the fire,
7.note: sn This escape refers to the exile of Ezekiel and others in 597 b.c. (Ezek 1:2; 2 Kgs 24:10-16).

8.anchor: I will make
8.note: tn The word translated “make” is the same Hebrew word translated as “provide” in v. 6.

1.anchor: In the eleventh year, on the first day of the month,
1.note: tc Date formulae typically include the month. According to D. I. Block (Ezekiel [NICOT], 2:34, n. 27) some emend to “in the twelfth year in the eleventh month” based partially on the copy of the LXX from Alexandrinus, where Albright suggested that “eleventh month” may have dropped out due to haplography.sn April 23, 587 b.c.

2.note: sn Tyre was located on the Mediterranean coast north of Israel.
map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

2.anchor: ‘Aha, the gateway of the peoples is broken; it has swung open to me. I will become rich,
2.note: tn Heb “I will be filled.”
sn That is, Jerusalem.

3.anchor: says: Look,
3.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) draws attention to something and has been translated here as a verb.

3.anchor: I am against you,
3.note: tn Or “I challenge you.” The phrase “I am against you” may be a formula for challenging someone to combat or a duel. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:201-2, and P. Humbert, “Die Herausforderungsformel ‘h!nn#n' ?l?K>,’” ZAW 45 (1933): 101-8. The Hebrew text switches to a second feminine singular form here, indicating that personified Jerusalem is addressed (see vv. 5-6a). The address to Jerusalem continues through v. 15. In vv. 16-17 the second masculine plural is used, as the people are addressed.

4.anchor: They will destroy the walls of Tyre and break down her towers. I will scrape her soil
4.note: tn Or “debris.”

6.anchor: and her daughters
6.note: sn That is, the towns located inland that were under Tyre’s rule.

7.anchor: says: Take note that
7.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) draws attention to something.

7.anchor: I am about to bring King Nebuchadrezzar
7.note: tn Heb “Nebuchadrezzar” is a variant and more correct spelling of Nebuchadnezzar, as the Babylonian name Nabu-kudurri-usur has an an “r” rather than an “n.”

9.anchor: He will direct the blows of his battering rams against your walls and tear down your towers with his weapons.
9.note: tn Heb “swords.”

10.anchor: He will cover you with the dust kicked up by his many horses.
10.note: tn Heb “From the abundance of his horses he will cover you (with) their dust.”

10.anchor: Your walls will shake from the noise of the horsemen, wheels, and chariots when he enters your gates like those who invade through a city’s broken walls.
10.note: tn Heb “like those who enter a breached city.”

12.anchor: They will steal your wealth and loot your merchandise. They will tear down your walls and destroy your luxurious
12.note: tn Heb “desirable.”

12.anchor: homes. Your stones, your trees, and your soil he will throw
12.note: tn Heb “set.”

12.anchor: into the water.
12.note: tn Heb “into the midst of the water.”

13.anchor: I will silence
13.note: tn Heb “cause to end.”

14.anchor: I will make you a bare rock; you will be a place where fishing nets are spread. You will never be built again,
14.note: sn This prophecy was fulfilled by Alexander the Great in 332 b.c.

16.anchor: All the princes of the sea will vacate
16.note: tn Heb “descend from.”

16.anchor: their thrones. They will remove their robes and strip off their embroidered clothes; they will clothe themselves with trembling. They will sit on the ground; they will tremble continually and be shocked at what has happened to you.
16.note: tn Heb “and they will be astonished over you.”

17.anchor: she and her inhabitants, who spread their terror!
17.note: tn Heb “she and her inhabitants who placed their terror to all her inhabitants.” The relationship of the final prepositional phrase to what precedes is unclear. The preposition probably has a specifying function here, drawing attention to Tyre’s inhabitants as the source of the terror mentioned prior to this. In this case, one might paraphrase verse 17b: “she and her inhabitants, who spread their terror; yes, her inhabitants (were the source of this terror).”

18.anchor: the coastlands by the sea will be terrified by your passing.’
18.note: tn Heb “from your going out.”

19.anchor: says: When I make you desolate like the uninhabited cities, when I bring up the deep over you and the surging
19.note: tn Heb “many.”

20.anchor: then I will bring you down to bygone people,
20.note: tn Heb “to the people of antiquity.”

20.anchor: to be with those who descend to the pit. I will make you live in the lower parts of the earth, among
20.note: tn Heb “like.” The translation assumes an emendation of the preposition כְּ (kÿ, “like”), to בְּ (bÿ, “in, among”).

20.anchor: the primeval ruins, with those who descend to the pit, so that you will not be inhabited or stand
20.note: tn Heb “and I will place beauty.” This reading makes little sense; many, following the lead of the LXX, emend the text to read “nor will you stand” with the negative particle before the preceding verb understood by ellipsis; see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:73. D. I. Block (Ezekiel [NICOT], 2:47) offers another alternative, taking the apparent first person verb form as an archaic second feminine form and translating “nor radiate splendor.”

1.anchor: In the tenth year, in the tenth month, on the twelfth day of the month,
1.note: tn January 7, 587 b.c.

2.note: tn Heb “set your face against.”

3.anchor: who has said, “My Nile is my own, I made it for myself.”
3.note: sn In Egyptian theology Pharaoh owned and controlled the Nile. See J. D. Currid, Ancient Egypt and the Old Testament, 240-44.

5.anchor: you will fall in the open field and will not be gathered up or collected.
5.note: tc Some Hebrew mss, the Targum, and the LXX read “buried.”

6.anchor: because they were a reed staff
6.note: sn Compare Isa 36:6.

7.anchor: and when they leaned on you, you splintered and caused their legs to be unsteady.
7.note: tn Heb “you caused to stand for them all their hips.” An emendation which switches two letters but is supported by the LXX yields the reading “you caused all their hips to shake.” See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:103. In 2 Kgs 18:21 and Isa 36:6 trusting in the Pharaoh is compared to leaning on a staff. The oracle may reflect Hophra’s attempt to aid Jerusalem (Jer 37:5-8).

8.anchor: says: Look, I am about to bring a sword against you, and I will kill
8.note: tn Heb “I will cut off from you.”

10.anchor: I am against
10.note: tn Or “I challenge you.” The phrase “I am against you” may be a formula for challenging someone to combat or a duel. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:201-2, and P. Humbert, “Die Herausforderungsformel ‘h!nn#n' ?l?K>,’” ZAW 45 (1933): 101-8.

10.anchor: you and your waterways. I will turn the land of Egypt into an utter desolate ruin from Migdol
10.note: sn This may refer to a site in the Egyptian Delta which served as a refuge for Jews (Jer 44:1; 46:14).

10.anchor: to Syene,
10.note: sn Syene is known today as Aswan.

13.anchor: says: At the end of forty years
13.note: sn In Ezek 4:4-8 it was said that the house of Judah would suffer forty years.

14.anchor: I will restore the fortunes of Egypt, and will bring them back
14.note: tc Thus the Masoretic Text. The LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate translate as though the Hebrew read “cause to inhabit.”

16.anchor: It will never again be Israel’s source of confidence, but a reminder of how they sinned by turning to Egypt for help.
16.note: tn Heb “reminding of iniquity when they turned after them.”

17.anchor: In the twenty-seventh year, in the first month, on the first day of the month,
17.note: sn April 26, 571 b.c.

18.anchor: “Son of man, King Nebuchadrezzar
18.note: tn Heb “Nebuchadrezzar” is a variant and more correct spelling of Nebuchadnezzar, as the Babylonian name Nabu-kudurri-usur has an “r” rather than an “n” (so also in v. 19).

18.anchor: of Babylon made his army labor hard against Tyre.
18.note: sn Nebuchadnezzar besieged Tyre from 585 to 571 b.c.map For location see Map1-A2; Map2-G2; Map4-A1; JP3-F3; JP4-F3.

20.anchor: I have given him the land of Egypt as his compensation for attacking Tyre
20.note: tn Heb “for which he worked,” referring to the assault on Tyre (v. 18).

21.anchor: On that day I will make Israel powerful,
21.note: tn Heb “I will cause a horn to sprout for the house of Israel.” The horn is used as a figure for military power in the OT (Ps 92:10). A similar expression is made about the Davidic dynasty in Ps 132:17.

21.anchor: and I will give you the right to be heard
21.note: tn Heb “I will grant you an open mouth.”

2.anchor: though you think you are godlike.
2.note: tn Heb “and you made your heart (mind) like the heart (mind) of gods.”

3.anchor: no secret is hidden from you.
3.note: sn The tone here is sarcastic, reflecting the ruler’s view of himself.

5.anchor: By your great skill
5.note: tn Or “wisdom.”

6.anchor: Because you think you are godlike,
6.note: tn Heb “because of your making your heart like the heart of gods.”

7.anchor: They will draw their swords against the grandeur made by your wisdom,
7.note: tn Heb “they will draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom.”

8.anchor: They will bring you down to the pit, and you will die violently
8.note: tn Heb “you will die the death of the slain.”

10.anchor: You will die the death of the uncircumcised
10.note: sn The Phoenicians practiced circumcision, so the language here must be figurative, indicating that they would be treated in a disgraceful manner. Uncircumcised peoples were viewed as inferior, unclean, and perhaps even sub-human. See 31:18 and 32:17-32, as well as the discussion in D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 2:99.

12.anchor: “‘You were the sealer
12.note: tn For a discussion of possible nuances of this phrase, see M. Greenberg, Ezekiel (AB), 2:580-81.

13.anchor: the sapphire, turquoise, and beryl;
13.note: tn The exact identification of each gemstone is uncertain. The list should be compared to that of the priest in Exod 28:17-20, which lists twelve stones in rows of three. The LXX apparently imports the Exod 28 list. See reference to the types of stones in L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:91.

14.anchor: I placed you there with an anointed
14.note: tn Or “winged”; see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:91.

14.anchor: guardian
14.note: tn The meaning of this phrase in Hebrew is uncertain. The word translated here “guards” occurs in Exod 25:20 in reference to the cherubim “covering” the ark.

14.anchor: cherub;
14.note: tn Heb “you (were) an anointed cherub that covers and I placed you.” In the Hebrew text the ruler of Tyre is equated with a cherub, and the verb “I placed you” is taken with what follows (“on the holy mountain of God”). However, this reading is problematic. The pronoun “you” at the beginning of verse 14 is feminine singular in the Hebrew text; elsewhere in this passage the ruler of Tyre is addressed with masculine singular forms. It is possible that the pronoun is a rare (see Deut 5:24; Num 11:15) or defectively written (see 1 Sam 24:19; Neh 9:6; Job 1:10; Ps 6:3; Eccl 7:22) masculine form, but it is more likely that the form should be repointed as the preposition “with” (see the LXX). In this case the ruler of Tyre is compared to the first man, not to a cherub. If this emendation is accepted, then the verb “I placed you” belongs with what precedes and concludes the first sentence in the verse. It is noteworthy that the verbs in the second and third lines of the verse also appear at the end of the sentence in the Hebrew text. The presence of a conjunction at the beginning of “I placed you” is problematic for the proposal, but it may reflect a later misunderstanding of the syntax of the verse. For a defense of the proposed emendation, see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:91.

15.anchor: You were blameless in your behavior
15.note: tn Heb “ways.”

16.anchor: the guardian cherub expelled you
16.note: tn Heb “and I expelled you, O guardian cherub.” The Hebrew text takes the verb as first person and understands “guardian cherub” as a vocative, in apposition to the pronominal suffix on the verb. However, if the emendation in verse 14a is accepted (see the note above), then one may follow the LXX here as well and emend the verb to a third person perfect. In this case the subject of the verb is the guardian cherub. See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:91.

21.anchor: “Son of man, turn toward
21.note: tn Heb “set your face against.”

21.anchor: Sidon
21.note: sn Sidon was located 25 miles north of Tyre.map For location see Map1-A1; JP3-F3; JP4-F3.

22.anchor: and reveal my sovereign power
22.note: tn Or “reveal my holiness.” God’s “holiness” is fundamentally his transcendence as sovereign ruler of the world. The revelation of his authority and power through judgment is in view in this context.

23.anchor: the slain will fall within it, by the sword that attacks it
23.note: tn Heb “by a sword against it.”

24.anchor: “‘No longer will Israel suffer from the sharp briers
24.note: sn Similar language is used in reference to Israel’s adversaries in Num 33:55; Josh 23:13.

24.anchor: or painful thorns of all who surround and scorn them.
24.note: tn Heb “and there will not be for the house of Israel a brier that pricks and a thorn that inflicts pain from all the ones who surround them, the ones who scorn them.”

25.anchor: says: When I regather the house of Israel from the peoples where they are dispersed, I will reveal my sovereign power
25.note: tn Or “reveal my holiness.” See verse 22.

26.anchor: They will live securely in it; they will build houses and plant vineyards. They will live securely
26.note: sn This promise was given in Lev 25:18-19.

2.note: tn Heb “Alas for the day.”

3.anchor: it will be a time of judgment
3.note: tn Heb “a time.” The words “of judgment” have been added in the translation for clarification (see the following verses).

5.anchor: Ethiopia, Put, Lud, all the foreigners,
5.note: tn The same expression appears in Exod 12:38; Jer 25:20; 50:37; Neh 13:3. It may refer to foreign mercenaries serving in the armies of the nations listed here.

5.anchor: Libya, and the people
5.note: tn Heb “sons.”

5.anchor: of the covenant land
5.note: tn The expression “sons of the covenant land” possibly refers to Jews living in Egypt (Jer 44).

6.anchor: From Migdol to Syene
6.note: sn Syene is known as Aswan today.

8.anchor: and all her allies are defeated.
8.note: tn Heb “all who aid her are broken.”

9.anchor: On that day messengers will go out from me in ships to frighten overly confident Ethiopia; panic will overtake them on the day of Egypt’s doom;
9.note: tn Heb “in the day of Egypt.” The word “doom” has been added in the translation to clarify the nature of this day.

10.anchor: by the hand of King Nebuchadrezzar
10.note: tn Heb “Nebuchadrezzar” is a variant and more correct spelling of Nebuchadnezzar, as the Babylonian name Nabu-kudurri-usur has an “r” rather than an “n.”

11.anchor: the most terrifying of the nations,
11.note: tn The Babylonians were known for their cruelty (2 Kgs 25:7).

12.anchor: and hand the land over to
12.note: tn Heb “and I will sell the land into the hand of.”

13.anchor: so I will make the land of Egypt fearful.
13.note: tn Heb “I will put fear in the land of Egypt.”

15.anchor: I will cut off
15.note: tn Or “kill.”

16.anchor: Syene
16.note: tc The LXX reads “Syene,” which is Aswan in the south. The MT reads Sin, which has already been mentioned in v. 15.

17.anchor: and the cities will go
17.note: tn Heb “they will go.” The pronoun and verb are feminine plural, indicating that the cities just mentioned are the antecedent of the pronoun and the subject of the verb. The translation makes this clear by stating the subject as “the cities.”

18.anchor: In Tahpanhes the day will be dark
18.note: sn In Zeph 1:15 darkness is associated with the day of the Lord.

20.anchor: In the eleventh year, in the first month, on the seventh day of the month,
20.note: tn April 29, 587 b.c.

21.anchor: “Son of man, I have broken the arm
21.note: sn The expression “breaking the arm” indicates the removal of power (Ps 10:15; 37:17; Job 38:15; Jer 48:25).

21.anchor: of Pharaoh king of Egypt.
21.note: sn This may refer to the event recorded in Jer 37:5.

22.anchor: says: Look,
22.note: tn The word h!nn@h indicates becoming aware of something and has been translated here as a verb.

22.anchor: I am against
22.note: tn Or “I challenge you.” The phrase “I am against you” may be a formula for challenging someone to combat or a duel. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:201-2, and P. Humbert, “Die Herausforderungsformel ‘h!nn#n' ?l?K>,’” ZAW 45 (1933): 101-8.

24.anchor: I will strengthen the arms of the king of Babylon, and I will place my sword in his hand, but I will break the arms of Pharaoh, and he will groan like the fatally wounded before the king of Babylon.
24.note: tn Heb “him”; the referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.

1.note: sn Ezek 21:1 in the English Bible is 21:6 in the Hebrew text (BHS). See the note at 20:45.

2.note: tn Heb “set your face toward.”
map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

3.anchor: and say to them,
3.note: tn Heb “the land of Israel.”

3.anchor: says: Look,
3.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) draws attention to something and has been translated here as a verb.

3.anchor: I am against you.
3.note: tn Or “I challenge you.” The phrase “I am against you” may be a formula for challenging someone to combat or a duel. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:201-2, and P. Humbert, “Die Herausforderungsformel ‘h!nn#n' ?l?K>,’” ZAW 45 (1933): 101-8.

3.anchor: I will draw my sword
3.note: sn This is the sword of judgment, see Isa 31:8; 34:6; 66:16.

3.anchor: from its sheath and cut off from you both the righteous and the wicked.
3.note: sn Ezekiel elsewhere pictures the Lord’s judgment as discriminating between the righteous and the wicked (9:4-6; 18:1-20; see as well Pss 1 and 11) and speaks of the preservation of a remnant (3:21; 6:8; 12:16). Perhaps here he exaggerates for rhetorical effect in an effort to subdue any false optimism. See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:25-26; D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:669-70; and W. Zimmerli, Ezekiel (Hermeneia), 1:424-25.

4.anchor: Because I will cut off from you both the righteous and the wicked, my sword will go out from its sheath against everyone
4.note: tn Heb “all flesh” (also in the following verse).

4.anchor: from the south
4.note: tn Heb “Negev.” The Negev is the south country.

6.anchor: “And you, son of man, groan with an aching heart
6.note: tn Heb “breaking loins.”

7.anchor: When they ask you, ‘Why are you groaning?’ you will reply, ‘Because of the report that has come. Every heart will melt with fear and every hand will be limp; everyone
7.note: tn Heb “every spirit will be dim.”

7.anchor: will faint and every knee will be wet with urine.’
7.note: sn This expression depicts in a very vivid way how they will be overcome with fear. See the note on the same phrase in 7:17.

10.anchor: “‘Should we rejoice in the scepter of my son? No! The sword despises every tree!
10.note: tn Heb “Or shall we rejoice, scepter of my son, it despises every tree.” The translation understands the subject of the verb “despises,” which is a feminine form in the Hebrew text, to be the sword (which is a feminine noun) mentioned just before this. Alternatively, the line may be understood as “let us not rejoice, O tribe of my son; it despises every tree.” The same word in Hebrew may be either “rod,” “scepter,” or “tribe.” The word sometimes translated as “or” or taken as an interrogative particle may be a negative particle. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:672, n. 79.sn The people of Judah should not place false hope in their king, symbolized by his royal scepter, for God’s judgment (symbolized by fire and then a sword) would destroy every tree (see 20:47), symbolizing the righteous and wicked (see 21:3-4).

12.anchor: Therefore, strike your thigh.
12.note: sn This physical action was part of an expression of grief. Cp. Jer. 31:19.

13.anchor: “‘For testing will come, and what will happen when the scepter, which the sword despises, is no more?
13.note: tn Heb “For testing (will come) and what if also a scepter, it despises, will not be?” The translation understands the subject of the verb “despises,” which is a feminine form in the Hebrew text, to be the sword (which is a feminine noun) mentioned in the previous verses. The text is very difficult and any rendering is uncertain.

16.anchor: wherever your edge
16.note: tn Heb “face.”

20.anchor: Mark out the routes for the sword to take: “Rabbah of the Ammonites” and “Judah with Jerusalem in it.”
20.note: tc The MT reads “Judah in fortified Jerusalem,” a geographic impossibility. The translation follows the LXX, which assumes בְּתוֹכָהּ (bÿtokhah, “in it”) for בְּצוּרָה (bÿtsurah, “fortified”). sn As the Babylonians approached from the north, one road would branch off to the left and lead down the east side of the Jordan River to Ammon. The other road would veer to the right and lead down west of the Jordan to Jerusalem.

21.anchor: For the king of Babylon stands at the fork
21.note: tn Heb “mother.”

21.anchor: in the road at the head of the two routes. He looks for omens:
21.note: sn Mesopotamian kings believed that the gods revealed the future through omens. They employed various divination techniques, some of which are included in the list that follows. A particularly popular technique was the examination and interpretation of the livers of animals. See R. R. Wilson, Prophecy and Society in Ancient Israel, 90-110.

21.anchor: He shakes arrows, he consults idols,
21.note: tn This word refers to personal idols that were apparently used for divination purposes (Gen 31:19; 1 Sam 19:13, 16).

21.anchor: he examines
21.note: tn Heb “sees.”

21.anchor: animal livers.
21.note: tn Heb “the liver.”

22.anchor: Into his right hand
22.note: tn Or “on the right side,” i.e., the omen mark on the right side of the liver.

22.anchor: comes the portent for Jerusalem – to set up battering rams, to give the signal
22.note: tn Heb “to open the mouth” for slaughter.

22.anchor: for slaughter, to shout out the battle cry,
22.note: tn Heb “to raise up a voice in a battle cry.”

23.anchor: But those in Jerusalem
23.note: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the people in Jerusalem) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

23.anchor: will view it as a false omen. They have sworn solemn oaths,
23.note: sn When the people of Judah realized the Babylonians’ intentions, they would object on grounds that they had made a treaty with the Babylonian king (see 17:13).

23.anchor: but the king of Babylon
23.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the king of Babylon) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

23.anchor: will accuse them of violations
23.note: tn Or “iniquity.”

23.anchor: in order to seize them.
23.note: tn Heb “and he will remind of guilt for the purpose of being captured.” The king would counter their objections by pointing out that they had violated their treaty with him (see 17:18).

24.anchor: says: ‘Because you have brought up
24.note: tn Heb “caused to be remembered.”

24.anchor: your own guilt by uncovering your transgressions and revealing your sins through all your actions, for this reason you will be taken by force.
24.note: tn Heb “Because you have brought to remembrance your guilt when your transgressions are uncovered so that your sins are revealed in all your deeds – because you are remembered, by the hand you will be seized.”

25.anchor: “‘As for you, profane and wicked prince of Israel,
25.note: tn This probably refers to King Zedekiah.

26.anchor: bring down the proud!
26.note: tn Heb “the high one.”

27.anchor: when the one arrives to whom I have assigned judgment.’
27.note: tn Heb “Also this, he was not, until the coming of the one to whom the judgment belongs and I have given it.” The Hebrew text, as it stands, is grammatically difficult. The pronoun “this” is feminine, while the following negated verb (“was not”) is masculine. Some emend the verb to a feminine form (see BHS). In this case the statement refers to the destiny of the king’s turban/crown (symbolizing his reign). See the previous note. The preposition translated “when” normally means “until,” but here it seems to refer to the period during which the preceding situation is realized, rather than its termination point. See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:19, 21. The second part of the statement, though awkward, probably refers to the arrival of the Babylonian king, to whom the Lord had assigned the task of judgment (see 23:24). Or the verse may read “A total ruin I will make, even this. It will not be until the one comes to whom is (the task of) judgment and I have assigned it.”

28.note: tn Heb “to contain, endure.” Since the Hebrew text as it stands makes little, if any, sense, most emend the text to read either “to consume” or “for destruction.” For discussion of options see D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:693.

29.note: tn Heb “you”; the referent (the sword mentioned in v. 28) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

29.anchor: on the necks of the profane wicked,
29.note: sn The second half of the verse appears to state that the sword of judgment would fall upon the wicked, despite their efforts to prevent it.

30.anchor: In the place where you were created,
30.note: tn In the Hebrew text of vv. 30-32 the second person verbal and pronominal forms are feminine singular. This may indicate that the personified Babylonian sword is being addressed. The Hebrew word for “sword” (see v. 28) is feminine. However, it may refer to the Ammonites.

32.anchor: your blood will stain the middle of the land;
32.note: tn Heb “your blood will be in the middle of the land.”

3.anchor: They engaged in prostitution in Egypt; in their youth they engaged in prostitution. Their breasts were squeezed there; lovers
3.note: tn In the Hebrew text the subject is left unstated and must be supplied from the context.

4.anchor: Oholah was the name of the older and Oholibah
4.note: tn The names Oholah and Oholibah are both derived from the word meaning “tent.” The meaning of Oholah is “her tent,” while Oholibah means “my tent is in her.”

4.anchor: the name of her younger sister. They became mine, and gave birth to sons and daughters.
4.note: sn In this allegory the Lord is depicted as being the husband of two wives. The OT law prohibited a man from marrying sisters (Lev 18:18), but the practice is attested in the OT (cf. Jacob). The metaphor is utilized here for illustrative purposes and does not mean that the Lord condoned such a practice or bigamy in general.

5.anchor: “Oholah engaged in prostitution while she was mine.
5.note: tn Heb “while she was under me.” The expression indicates that Oholah is viewed as the Lord’s wife. See Num 5:19-20, 29. sn Played the harlot refers to alliances with pagan nations in this context. In Ezek 16 harlotry described the sin of idolatry.

5.anchor: She lusted after her lovers, the Assyrians
5.note: tn Heb “Assyria.”

5.anchor: – warriors
5.note: tn The term apparently refers to Assyrian military officers; it is better construed with the description that follows. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 1:738.

7.anchor: She bestowed her sexual favors on them; all of them were the choicest young men of Assyria. She defiled herself with all whom she desired
7.note: tn Heb “lusted after.”

8.anchor: She did not abandon the prostitution she had practiced in Egypt; for in her youth men had sex with her, fondled her virgin breasts, and ravished her.
8.note: tn Heb “and poured out their harlotry on her.”

9.anchor: Therefore I handed her over to her lovers, the Assyrians
9.note: tn Heb “I gave her into the hand of her lovers, into the hand of the sons of Assyria.”

10.anchor: They exposed her nakedness, seized her sons and daughters, and killed her with the sword. She became notorious
10.note: tn Heb “name.”

11.anchor: “Her sister Oholibah watched this,
11.note: tn The word “this” is not in the original text.

14.anchor: But she increased her prostitution. She saw men carved on the wall, images of the Chaldeans carved in bright red,
14.note: tn The only other occurrence of the Hebrew term is in Jer 22:14.

15.anchor: wearing belts on their waists and flowing turbans on their heads, all of them looking like officers, the image of Babylonians
15.note: tn Heb “the sons of Babel.”

16.anchor: When she saw them,
16.note: tn Heb “at the appearance of her eyes.”

16.anchor: she lusted after them and sent messengers to them in Chaldea.
16.note: sn The Chaldeans were prominent tribal groups of Babylonia. The imagery is reminiscent of events in the reigns of Hezekiah (2 Kgs 20:12-15) and Jehoiakim (2 Kgs 23:34-24:1).

17.anchor: The Babylonians crawled into bed with her.
17.note: tn Heb “The sons of Babel came to her on a bed of love.”

17.anchor: They defiled her with their lust; after she was defiled by them, she
17.note: tn Heb “her soul.”

18.anchor: When she lustfully exposed her nakedness,
18.note: tn Heb “She exposed her harlotry and she exposed her nakedness.”

18.anchor: I
18.note: tn Heb “my soul.”

18.anchor: was disgusted with her, just as I
18.note: tn Heb “my soul.”

20.anchor: She lusted after their genitals – as large as those of donkeys,
20.note: tn Heb “She lusted after their concubines (?) whose flesh was the flesh of donkeys.” The phrase “their concubines” is extremely problematic here. The pronoun is masculine plural, suggesting that the Egyptian men are in view, but how concubines would fit into the picture envisioned here is not clear. Some suggest that Ezekiel uses the term in an idiomatic sense of “paramour,” but this still fails to explain how the pronoun relates to the noun. It is more likely that the term refers here to the Egyptians’ genitals. The relative pronoun that follows introduces a more specific description of their genitals.

21.anchor: This is how you assessed
21.note: tn Or “you took note of.” The Hebrew verb פָּקַד (paqad) in the Qal implies evaluating something and then acting in light of that judgment; here the prophet depicts Judah as approving of her youthful unfaithfulness and then magnifying it at the present time. Some translations assume the verb should be repointed as a Niphal, rendering “you missed” or by extension “you longed for,” but such an extension of the Niphal “to be missing” is otherwise unattested.

21.anchor: the obscene conduct of your youth, when the Egyptians fondled
21.note: tn Heb “when (they) did,” but the verb makes no sense here and is better emended to “when (they) fondled,” a verb used in vv. 3 and 8. See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:43.

21.anchor: your nipples and squeezed
21.note: tn Heb “for the sake of,” but the expression is awkward and is better emended to read “to squeeze.” See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:43.

22.anchor: says: Look here,
22.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates becoming aware of something and has been translated here as a verb.

23.anchor: the Babylonians and all the Chaldeans, Pekod,
23.note: sn Pekod was the name of an Aramean tribe (known as Puqudu in Mesopotamian texts) that lived in the region of the Tigris River.

23.anchor: Shoa,
23.note: sn Shoa was the name of a nomadic people (the Sutu) that lived in Mesopotamia.

23.anchor: and Koa,
23.note: sn Koa was the name of another Mesopotamian people group (the Qutu).

24.anchor: They will attack
24.note: tn Heb “come against.”

24.anchor: you with weapons,
24.note: tn This is the only occurrence of this term in the OT. The precise meaning is uncertain.

24.anchor: chariots, wagons, and with a huge army;
24.note: tn Heb “an assembly of peoples.”

24.anchor: they will array themselves against you on every side with large shields, small shields, and helmets. I will assign them the task of judgment;
24.note: tn Heb “I will place before them judgment.”

25.anchor: I will direct
25.note: tn Heb “give.”

25.anchor: my jealous anger against you, and they will deal with you in rage. They will cut off your nose and your ears,
25.note: tn Heb “they will remove.” sn This method of punishment is attested among ancient Egyptian and Hittite civilizations. See W. Zimmerli, Ezekiel (Hermeneia), 1:489.

25.anchor: and your survivors will die
25.note: tn Heb “fall.”

27.anchor: So I will put an end to your obscene conduct and your prostitution which you have practiced in the land of Egypt.
27.note: tn Heb “I will cause your obscene conduct to cease from you and your harlotry from the land of Egypt.”

27.anchor: You will not seek their help
27.note: tn Heb “lift your eyes to them.”

28.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates becoming aware of something and has been translated here as a verb.

28.anchor: I am about to deliver you over to
28.note: tn Heb “I am giving you into the hand of.”

29.anchor: They will treat you with hatred, take away all you have labored for,
29.note: tn The Hebrew term means “labor,” but by extension it can also refer to that for which one works.

29.anchor: and leave you naked and bare. Your nakedness will be exposed, just as when you engaged in prostitution and obscene conduct.
29.note: tn Heb “The nakedness of your prostitution will be exposed, and your obscene conduct and your harlotry.”

30.anchor: I will do these things to you
30.note: tn The infinitive absolute continues the sequence begun in v. 28: “Look here, I am about to deliver you.” See Joüon 2:430 §123.w.

31.anchor: You have followed the ways of your sister, so I will place her cup of judgment
31.note: tn Heb “her cup.” A cup of intoxicating strong drink is used, here and elsewhere, as a metaphor for judgment because both leave one confused and reeling. (See Jer 25:15, 17, 28; Hab 2:16.) The cup of wrath is a theme also found in the NT (Mark 14:36).

32.anchor: says: “You will drink your sister’s deep and wide cup;
32.note: sn The image of a deep and wide cup suggests the degree of punishment; it will be extensive and leave the victim helpless.

34.note: tn Heb “You will drink it and drain (it).”

tn D. I. Block compares this to the idiom of “licking the plate” (Ezekiel [NICOT], 1:754, n. 137). The text is difficult as the word translated “gnaw” is rare. The noun is used of the shattered pieces of pottery and so could envision a broken cup. But the Piel verb form is used in only one other place (Num 24:8), where it is a denominative from the noun “bone” and seems to mean to “break (bones).” Why it would be collocated with “sherds” is not clear. For this reason some emend the phrase to read “consume its dregs” (see L. C. Allen, Ezekiel [WBC], 2:44) or emend the verb to read “swallow,” as if the intoxicated Oholibah breaks the cup and then eats the very sherds in an effort to get every last drop of the beverage that dampens them.
sn The severe action is more extreme than beating the breasts in anguish (Isa 32:12; Nah 2:7). It is also ironic for these are the very breasts she so blatantly offered to her lovers (vv. 3, 21).

35.anchor: says: Because you have forgotten me and completely disregarded me,
35.note: tn Heb “and you cast me behind your back.” The expression pictures her rejection of the Lord (see 1 Kgs 14:9).

35.anchor: you must bear now the punishment
35.note: tn The word “punishment” is not in the Hebrew text but is demanded by the context.

36.anchor: said to me: “Son of man, are you willing to pronounce judgment
36.note: tn Heb “will you judge.” Here the imperfect form of the verb is probably used with a desiderative nuance. Addressed to the prophet, “judge” means to warn of or pronounce God’s impending judgment. See 20:4; 22:2.

37.anchor: For they have committed adultery and blood is on their hands. They have committed adultery with their idols, and their sons, whom they bore to me,
37.note: sn The Lord speaks here in the role of the husband of the sisters.

37.anchor: they have passed through the fire as food to their idols.
37.note: tn Heb “they have passed to them for food.” The verb is commonly taken to refer to passing children through fire, especially as an offering to the pagan god Molech. See Jer 32:35.

38.anchor: Moreover, they have done this to me: In the very same day

40.anchor: “They even sent for men from far away; when the messenger arrived, those men set out.
40.note: tn Heb “to whom a messenger was sent, and look, they came.” Foreign alliances are in view here.

tn The Hebrew verb form is feminine singular, indicating that Oholibah (Judah) is specifically addressed here. This address continues through verse 42a (note “her”), but then both sisters are described in verse 42b, where the feminine pronouns are again plural.

42.anchor: The sound of a carefree crowd accompanied her,

42.note: tn Heb “and men from the multitude of mankind.”
tn An alternate reading is “drunkards.” Sheba is located in the area of modern day Yemen.

42.anchor: were brought from the desert. The sisters
42.note: tn Heb “they”; the referents (the sisters) have been specified in the translation for the sake of clarity.

44.note: tn Heb “and they came to her.”

45.anchor: But upright men will punish them appropriately for their adultery and bloodshed,
45.note: tn Heb “and upright men will judge them (with) the judgment of adulteresses and the judgment of those who shed blood.”

47.anchor: That army will pelt them with stones and slash them with their swords; they will kill their sons and daughters and burn their houses.
47.note: tn The Hebrew text adds “with fire.”

49.anchor: They will repay you for your obscene conduct, and you will be punished for idol worship.
49.note: tn Heb “and the sins of your idols you will bear.” By extension it can mean the punishment for the sins.

1.anchor: In the eleventh year, in the third month, on the first day of the month,
1.note: sn June 21, 587 b.c.

3.anchor: Consider Assyria,
3.note: sn Either Egypt, or the Lord compares Egypt to Assyria, which is described in vv. 3-17 through the metaphor of a majestic tree. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 2:185. Like Egypt, Assyria had been a great world power, but in time God brought the Assyrians down. Egypt should learn from history the lesson that no nation, no matter how powerful, can withstand the judgment of God. Rather than following the text here, some prefer to emend the proper name Assyria to a similar sounding common noun meaning “boxwood” (see Ezek 27:6), which would make a fitting parallel to “cedar of Lebanon” in the following line. In this case vv. 3-18 in their entirety refer to Egypt, not Assyria. See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:121-27.

3.anchor: a cedar in Lebanon,
3.note: sn Lebanon was know for its cedar trees (Judg 9:15; 1 Kgs 4:33; 5:6; 2 Kgs 14:9; Ezra 3:7; Pss 29:5; 92:12; 104:16).

4.anchor: while smaller channels watered all the trees of the field.
4.note: tn Heb “Waters made it grow; the deep made it grow tall. It (the deep) was flowing with its rivers around the place it (the tree) was planted, it (the deep) sent out its channels to all the trees of the field.”

5.anchor: because of the plentiful water in its shoots.
5.note: tn Heb “when it sends forth.” Repointing the consonants of the Masoretic text would render the proposed reading “shoots” (cf. NRSV).

6.anchor: in its shade all the great
6.note: tn Or “many.”

8.anchor: nor could the fir trees
8.note: tn Or “cypress trees” (cf. NASB, NLT); NIV “pine trees.”

11.anchor: I gave it over to the leader of the nations. He has judged it thoroughly,
11.note: tn Heb “acting he has acted with regard to it.” The infinitive absolute precedes the main verb to emphasize the certainty and decisiveness of the action depicted.

12.anchor: Foreigners from the most terrifying nations have cut it down and left it to lie there on the mountains. In all the valleys its branches have fallen, and its boughs lie broken in the ravines of the land. All the peoples of the land
12.note: tn Or “earth” (KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).

12.anchor: have departed
12.note: tn Heb “gone down.”

13.anchor: On its ruins all the birds of the sky will live, and all the wild animals
13.note: tn Heb “the beasts of the field,” referring to wild as opposed to domesticated animals.

13.anchor: will walk
13.note: tn Heb “be.”

14.anchor: For this reason no watered trees will grow so tall; their tops will not reach into the clouds, nor will the well-watered ones grow that high.
14.note: tn Heb “and they will not stand to them in their height, all the drinkers of water.”

14.anchor: For all of them have been appointed to die in the lower parts of the earth;
14.note: tn Heb “for death, to the lower earth.”

14.anchor: they will be among mere mortals,
14.note: tn Heb “the sons of men.”

15.anchor: says: On the day it
15.note: tn Or “he.”

15.anchor: went down to Sheol I caused observers to lament.
15.note: tn Heb “I caused lamentation.” D. I. Block (Ezekiel [NICOT], 2:194-95) proposes an alternative root which would give the meaning “I gated back the waters,” i.e., shut off the water supply.

16.anchor: I made the nations shake at the sound of its fall, when I threw it down to Sheol, along with those who descend to the pit.
16.note: sn For the expression “going down to the pit,” see Ezek 26:20; 32:18, 24, 29.

17.anchor: Those who lived in its shade, its allies
17.note: tn Heb “its arm.”

1.anchor: In the twelfth year, in the twelfth month, on the first of the month,
1.note: sn This would be March 3, 585 b.c.

2.note: tc The Hebrew reads “their streams”; the LXX reads “your streams.”

3.anchor: “‘I will throw my net over you
3.note: tn The expression “throw my net” is common in Ezekiel (12:13; 17:20; 19:8).

4.anchor: and I will permit
4.note: tn Or “cause.”

4.anchor: all the wild animals
4.note: tn Heb “the beasts of the field,” referring to wild as opposed to domesticated animals.

5.anchor: and fill the valleys with your maggot-infested carcass.
5.note: tc The Hebrew text is difficult here, apparently meaning “your height.” Following Symmachus and the Syriac, it is preferable to emend the text to read “your maggots.” See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 2:203.

6.anchor: and the ravines will be full of your blood.
6.note: tn Heb “from you.”

7.anchor: and the moon will not shine.
7.note: tn Heb “will not shine its light.” For similar features of cosmic eschatology, see Joel 2:10; 4:15; Amos 5:18-20; Zeph 1:5.

9.anchor: I will disturb
9.note: tn Heb “I will provoke the heart of.”

11.anchor: “‘The sword of the king of Babylon
11.note: sn The king of Babylon referred to here was Nebuchadnezzar (Ezek 21:19).

11.anchor: will attack
11.note: tn Heb “approach.”

13.anchor: and no human foot will disturb
13.note: tn Heb “muddy.”

13.anchor: the waters
13.note: tn Heb “them,” that is, the waters mentioned in the previous line. The translation clarifies the referent.

14.anchor: Then I will make their waters calm,
14.note: tn Heb “sink,” that is, to settle and become clear, not muddied.

17.anchor: In the twelfth year, on the fifteenth day of the month,
17.note: tn March 17, 585 b.c. The LXX adds “first month.”

18.anchor: “Son of man, wail
18.note: tn The Hebrew verb is used as a response to death (Jer 9:17-19; Amos 5:16).

18.anchor: over the horde of Egypt. Bring it down;
18.note: sn Through this prophetic lament given by God himself, the prophet activates the judgment described therein. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 2:217, and L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:136-37.

18.anchor: bring
18.note: tn Heb “Bring him down, her and the daughters of the powerful nations, to the earth below.” The verb “bring down” appears in the Hebrew text only once. Because the verb takes several objects here, the repetition of the verb in the translation improves the English style.

18.anchor: her
18.note: tn This apparently refers to personified Egypt.

19.anchor: Say to them,
19.note: tc The LXX places this verse after v. 21.tn The words “say to them” are added in the translation for clarity to indicate the shift in addressee from the prophet to Egypt.

19.anchor: ‘Whom do you surpass in beauty?
19.note: tn Heb “pleasantness.”

22.anchor: “Assyria is there with all her assembly around her grave,
22.note: tn Heb “around him his graves.” The masculine pronominal suffixes are problematic; the expression is best emended to correspond to the phrase “around her grave” in v. 23. See D. I. Block, Ezekiel (NICOT), 2:219.

22.anchor: all of them struck down by the sword.
22.note: tn Heb “all of them slain, the ones felled by the sword.” See as well vv. 23-24.

23.anchor: Their
23.note: tn Heb “whose.”

23.anchor: graves are located in the remote slopes of the pit.
23.note: tn The only other occurrence of the phrase “remote slopes of the pit” is in Isa 14:15.

25.anchor: Among the dead they have made a bed for her, along with all her hordes around her grave.
25.note: tn Heb “around him her graves,” but the expression is best emended to read “around her grave” (see vv. 23-24).

26.anchor: “Meshech-Tubal is there, along with all her hordes around her grave.
26.note: tn Heb “around him her graves,” but the expression is best emended to read “around her grave” (see vv. 23-24).

27.anchor: They do not lie with the fallen warriors of ancient times,
27.note: tc Heb “of the uncircumcised.” The LXX reads, probably correctly, “from of old” rather than “of the uncircumcised.” The phrases are very similar in spelling. The warriors of Meshech-Tubal are described as uncircumcised, so it would be odd for them to not be buried with the uncircumcised. Verse 28 specifically says that they would lie with the uncircumcised.

27.anchor: who went down to Sheol with their weapons of war, having their swords placed under their heads and their shields on their bones,
27.note: tn Heb “and their iniquities were over their bones.” The meaning of this statement is unclear; in light of the parallelism (see “swords”) it is preferable to emend “their iniquities” to “their swords.” See L. C. Allen, Ezekiel (WBC), 2:135.

2.note: tn Heb “set your face toward.”
tn Heb “the sons of Ammon.” Ammon was located to the east of Israel.

4.anchor: So take note,
4.note: tn The word הִנֵּה (hinneh, traditionally “behold”) indicates being aware of or taking notice of something and has been translated here with a verb (so also throughout the chapter).

4.anchor: I am about to make you slaves of
4.note: tn Heb “Look I am about to give you for a possession to.”

4.anchor: the tribes
4.note: tn Heb “sons.”

5.anchor: I will make Rabbah a pasture for camels and Ammon
5.note: tn Heb “the sons of Ammon.”

6.anchor: says: Because you clapped your hands, stamped your feet, and rejoiced with intense scorn
6.note: tn Heb “with all your scorn in (the) soul.”

7.anchor: take note, I have stretched out my hand against you, and I will hand you over as plunder
7.note: tc The translation here follows the marginal reading (Qere) of the Hebrew text. The consonantal text (Kethib) is meaningless.

8.anchor: says: ‘Moab
8.note: sn Moab was located immediately south of Ammon.

9.anchor: So look, I am about to open up Moab’s flank,
9.note: tn Heb “shoulder.”

9.anchor: eliminating the cities,
9.note: tn Heb “from the cities.” The verb “eliminating” has been added in the translation to reflect the privative use of the preposition (see BDB 583 s.v. מִן 7.b).

9.anchor: including its frontier cities,
9.note: tn Heb “from its cities, from its end.”

10.anchor: I will hand it over,
10.note: tn Heb “I will give it for a possession.”

10.anchor: along with the Ammonites,
10.note: tn Heb “the sons of Ammon” (twice in this verse).

10.anchor: to the tribes
10.note: tn Heb “the sons.”

12.anchor: says: ‘Edom
12.note: sn Edom was located south of Moab.

12.anchor: has taken vengeance against the house of Judah; they have made themselves fully culpable
12.note: tn Heb “and they have become guilty, becoming guilty.” The infinitive absolute following the finite verb makes the statement emphatic and draws attention to the degree of guilt incurred by Edom due to its actions.

12.anchor: by taking vengeance
12.note: tn Heb “and they have taken vengeance.”

12.anchor: on them.
12.note: sn Edom apparently in some way assisted in the destruction of Jerusalem in 587/6 b.c. (Ps 137:7; Lam 5:21, 23; Joel 3:19; Obadiah).

7.anchor: , the Holy One of Israel.
7.note: sn The basic sense of the word “holy” is “set apart from that which is commonplace, special, unique.” The Lord’s holiness is first and foremost his transcendent sovereignty as the ruler of the world. He is “set apart” from the world over which he rules. At the same time his holiness encompasses his moral authority, which derives from his royal position. As king he has the right to dictate to his subjects how they are to live; indeed his very own character sets the standard for proper behavior. This expression is a common title for the Lord in the book of Isaiah.
